# Swami Vivekananda's Places — Research from the Complete Works

*References and passages extracted from the Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda.*

*Each entry includes: period of visit, significance, and relevant passages/quotes from the Complete Works.*


---

## Contents

- **Early Life & Indian Wandering (1863–1893)** — 118 locations
- **First Western Tour & Parliament of Religions (1893–1897)** — 195 locations
- **Return to India & Mission Building (1897–1899)** — 90 locations
- **Second Western Tour (1899–1900)** — 23 locations
- **Final Years in India (1900–1902)** — 7 locations

---


## Phase: Early Life & Indian Wandering (1863–1893)

*Born in Kolkata, spiritual awakening under Sri Ramakrishna, and six years of wandering as a parivrajaka (wandering monk) across the length and breadth of India — discovering the land, its people, and crystallising his vision for India's spiritual renaissance.*


---

### Birthplace of Vivekananda

**Location:** 3, Gourmohan Mukherjee Street, Simla, Kolkata, India
**Period:** 12 January 1863
**Significance:** Birthplace


Narendranath Datta — who would become Swami Vivekananda — was born here on 12 January 1863, on the eve of Makar Sankranti. The house belonged to his father Vishwanath Datta.

### Balaram Bose's House

**Location:** 57, Ramkanta Bose Street, Kolkata, India
**Period:** 1882–1886
**Significance:** Major gathering place


Balaram Bose was one of Sri Ramakrishna's most devoted householder disciples. His home was a regular meeting place for Ramakrishna and his young disciples, including Narendra.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references found):

> \
> Complete Works, Vol. V. ) \
> \
> \
> [Place: Calcutta. Year: 1897.] \
> \
> For some days past, Swamiji has been staying at Balaram Bose's house, Baghbazar. There will be a total eclipse of \
> the sun today. The disciple is to cook for Swamiji this morning, and on his presenting himself, Swamiji said, "Well, \
> the cooking must be in the East Bengal style; and we must finish our dinner before the eclipse starts." \
> \
> The inner apartments of the house were all unoccupied now. So the disciple went inside into the kitchen and started \
> his cooking. Swamiji also was looking in now and then with a word of encouragement and sometimes with a joke, \
> as, "Take care, the soup (The Bengali expression has a…

### Ramakrishna Math, Baranagar

**Location:** Baranagar, Kolkata, India
**Period:** 1886–1892
**Significance:** First monastery


After Ramakrishna's passing in 1886, the young disciples rented this dilapidated house and established the first Ramakrishna Math. Narendra and his brothers lived here as renunciates, conducting rigorous spiritual practice.

### Alambazar Math

**Location:** Alambazar, Kolkata, India
**Period:** 1892–1898
**Significance:** Second monastery


The Math was relocated to Alambazar in 1892 and remained here until the permanent Belur Math was established. Vivekananda returned here after the Parliament of Religions and launched the Ramakrishna Mission.


**From the Complete Works** (17 references found):

> \
> (The disciple is Sharatchandra Chakravarty, who published his records in a Bengali book, Swami-Shishya-Samvada, in two parts. The present \

> \'ab \
> \
> THOUGHTS ON THE GITA \
> \
> During his sojourn in Calcutta in 1897, Swami Vivekananda used to stay for the most part at the Math, the \
> headquarters of the Ramakrisnna Mission, located then at Alambazar. During this time several young men, who \
> had been preparing themselves for some time previously, gathered round him and took the vows of Brahmacharya \
> and Sannyasa, and Swamiji began to train them for future work, by holding classes on the Gita and Vedanta, and \
> initiating them into the practices of meditation. In one of these classes he talked eloquently in Bengali on the Gita. \
> The following is the translation of the summary of the discourse as it was entered in the Math…

### Ghusuree Village

**Location:** Ghusuree, Bally, Howrah, India
**Period:** 1890
**Significance:** Meditation retreat


A retreat house used by Vivekananda and his brother disciples for meditation and spiritual practice during his early wandering years.


**From the Complete Works** (3 references found):

> standing posture; that boat of merchant Shrimanta's voyage (according to Kavikankan, Shrimanta crossed the Bay \
> of Bengal simply by rowing, and was about to be drowned owing to his boat getting caught in the antennae of a \
> shoal of lobsters, and almost capsizing! Also he mistook a shell for a tiny fish, and so on), in other words the \
> Gangasagar boat \'97 nicely roofed above and having a floor of split bamboos, and containing in its hold rows of \
> jars filled with Ganga water (which is deliciously cool, I beg your pardon, you visit Gangasagar during hard winter, \
> and the chill north wind drives away all your relish for cooling drinks); and that small-sized boat which daily takes \
> the…

> under the violent rush of a suddenly aroused mass of past recollections \'97 is the fear in his mind that now stands \
> foremost in the way of his visit to this country. Still as a worldly man, whoever he may be, he has to look to all \
> sides and conduct himself accordingly. When, after a complete surrender of all worldly interests, even the \
> Sannyasin, when perfonning any practices which he knows to be purest in themselves, is seen to shiver in fear of \
> public opinion, simply because they are held with disapproval by the people among whom he lives; when the \
> consideration of gaining name and fame and high position, and the fear of losing them regulate the actions of even \
> the greatest…

> \
> I would, therefore, suggest that you start after breakfast and remain the day here and have some Bengalee fish \
> lunch, and go back in the evening. \
> \
> I insist on your taking a cab here and back. A cab to come and go costs quite as much or less than a boat, and there \
> is no change [of transportation]. If the cabby does not understand Belur, tell him to go to a place two miles south of \
> Bally. He must know Bally, and then let him ask his way to the Math. \
> \
> One such drenching and capsizing experience as Mr. Okakura [Kakuzo] had the other day will unsettle your nerves \
> for days; and we expect such rough weather every evening this month. The land route is nearer, easier, and cheaper…


---

### Rajballavpara

**Location:** Rajballavpara Lane, Kolkata, India
**Period:** 1884
**Significance:** Residence


The Datta family moved to Rajballavpara Lane after financial difficulties. Vivekananda experienced extreme poverty here following his father's death in 1884, yet never lost his spiritual focus.

### Gopal Lal Seal's House

**Location:** Shyambazar, Kolkata, India
**Period:** 1891
**Significance:** Departure point


Vivekananda received his passage to the Parliament of World's Religions, partly funded through the efforts of patrons including the Maharaja of Khetri. Associated with final preparations before his western journey.

### Dakshineswar Kali Temple

**Location:** Dakshineswar, Kolkata, India
**Period:** 1881–1886
**Significance:** Spiritual awakening


It was here that the young Narendra first met Sri Ramakrishna in 1881. The temple complex on the Ganges was Ramakrishna's home for over thirty years. Narendra's repeated visits here transformed him into a true spiritual seeker.


**From the Complete Works** (11 references found):

> \
> (The disciple is Sharatchandra Chakravarty, who published his records in a Bengali book, Swami-Shishya-Samvada, in two parts. The present \
> series of "Conversations and Dialogues" is a revised translation from this book. Five dialogues of this series have already appeared in the \
> \
> Complete Works, Vol. V. ) \

> knowledge India is laid prostrate at their feet! \
> \
> Swamiji: Don't talk nonsense, my dear, hear what I say. India will have to carry others' shoes for ever on her head \
> if the charge of imparting knowledge to her sons does not again fall upon the shoulders of Tyagis. Don\'92t you know \
> how an illiterate boy, possessed of renunciation, turned the heads of your great old Pandits? Once at the \
> Dakshineswar Temple the Brahmana who was in charge of the worship of Vishnu broke a leg of the image. Pandits \
> were brought together at a meeting to give their opinions, and they, after…

> was, first, his literary education, in Sanskrit and English. The contrast between the two worlds thus opened to him \
> carried with it a strong impression of that particular experience which fonned the theme of the Indian sacred books. \

> accident. Rather was it the subject-matter of a science, the object of a logical analysis that shrank from no sacrifice \
> which the pursuit of truth demanded. \
> \
> In his Master, Ramakrishna Paramahamsa, living and teaching in the temple-garden at Dakshineshwar, the Swami \
> Vivekananda \'97 "Naren" as he then was \'97 found that verification…

> Swamiji: That also can be done. Look here, a time comes when what you call differentiation vanishes, and we \
> cannot perceive it at all. I have experienced that state in my own life. \
> \
> Disciple: When have you done so? \
> \
> Swamiji: One day in the temple-garden at Dakshineswar Shri Ramakrishna touched me over the heart, and first of \
> all I began to see that the houses \'97 rooms, doors, windows, verandahs \'97 the trees, the sun, the moon \'97 all were \
> flying off, shattering to pieces as it were \'97 reduced to atoms and molecules \'97 and ultimately became merged in \
> the Akasha. Gradually again, the Akasha also vanished, and after that, my consciousness of the ego with it; what…


---

### Kamarpukur

**Location:** Kamarpukur village, Hooghly, India
**Period:** 1887
**Significance:** Ramakrishna's birthplace


Birthplace of Sri Ramakrishna. Vivekananda and brother disciples visited here to pay homage after Ramakrishna's passing, experiencing firsthand the village environment that shaped their master's life.


**From the Complete Works** (7 references found):

> for their ships to come up. The site afterwards developed into the famous town of Hooghly. From the \
> commencement of the sixteenth century both Indian and foreign merchants were feeling much anxiety about the \
> silting up of the Ganga. But what of that? Human engineering skill has hitherto proved ineffectual against the \
> gradual silting up of the river-bed which continues to the present day. In 1666 a French Missionary writes that the \
> Ganga near Suti got completely silted up at the time. Holwell, of Black-Hole fame, on his way to Murshidabad was \
> compelled to resort to small country-boats on account of the shallowness of the river at Santipur. In 1797 Captain \
> Colebrook writes that…

> "May one ask if you originated this religion you have come to preach to England?" \
> \
> "Certainly not. I am a pupil of a great Indian sage, Ramakrishna Paramahamsa. He was not what one might call a \
> very learned man, as some of our sages are, but a very holy one, deeply imbued with the spirit of the Vedanta \
> philosophy. When I say philosophy, I hardly kn ow whether I ought not to say religion, for it is really both. You \
> must read Professor Max Muller's account of my Master in a recent number of the Nineteenth Century. \
> Ramakrishna was bom in the Hooghly district in 1836 and died in 1886. He produced a deep effect on the life of \
> Keshab Chandra Sen and others. By discipline of the body…

> \
> \
> PS. My heart leaps with joy \'97 and it is a wonder that I do not go mad when I find anybody thoroughly launched \
> into the midst of the doctrine which is to shower peace on earth hereafter. \
> \
> \
> *A village in the Hooghly District, the birth-place of Swami Premananda. The letter was written to Master Mahashaya. \

> begin. And there is talk about another danger. Even near Calcutta, through earthquakes or other causes, the river at \
> times dried up so much that one could wade across. It is said that in 1770 such a state of things happened. There is \
> another report that on Thursday, the 9th October, 1734, during ebb-tide in the noon, the river dried up completely. \
> Had it happened a little later, during the inauspicious last portion of the day, I leave it to you to infer the result. \
> Perhaps then the river would not have returned to its bed again. \
> \
> So far, then, as regards the upper portion of the Hooghly; now as regards the portion below Calcutta. The great \
> dangers to be faced in this portion…

**Location:** Antpur village, Hooghly, India
**Period:** December 1886
**Significance:** Monastic vows


During Christmas Eve 1886, Narendra and his brother disciples informally took monastic vows here at a midnight fire ritual, inspired by the life of Jesus Christ — marking the birth of the Ramakrishna monastic order.

One of the twelve Jyotirlinga shrines of Shiva. Vivekananda visited Baidyanath during his wandering years, experiencing the intense devotion of pilgrims and deepening his understanding of India's religious traditions.

> excellent so far as the air is concerned, but the water is not good, it upsets the stomach. I used to suffer from acidity \

> climate \'97 specially the quarter I am living in. I have visited Pavhari Baba's house \'97 there are high walls all round, \

> Vrindaban, \
> 12th Aug., 1888. \
> \
> \
> Leaving Ayodhya I have reached the holy Vrindaban, and am putting up at Kala Babu's Kunja. In the town the \
> mind feels contracted. Places like Radha-kunda, I have heard, are delightful; but they are at some distance from the \
> town. I have a mind to proceed very shortly to Hardwar. In case you have any acquaintance there, you would be \
> doing me a great favour if you would kindly write him an introduction for me. What about your visiting this place? \
> Please reply early and oblige. \
> \
> \
> Yours etc., \
> \

> \
> \
> An aged brother-disciple of mine who has just come back to Vrindaban after visiting Kedarnath and Badrinath met \
> Gangadhar. Twice did Gangadhar ascend up to Tibet and Bhutan. He is in great happiness and felt overwhelmed \
> and wept at the meeting. He spent the winter at Kankhal. The Karoa (waterpot) you gave him, he still keeps with \
> him. He is coming back and is expected at Vrindaban this very month. So in the hope of meeting him, I postpone \
> my going to Hardwar for some days. Please convey my deepest respects to the Brahmin devotee of Shiva who is \
> with you and accept the same yourself. \
> \
> \
> Yours etc., \
> \
> VlVEKANANDA. \

### Hrishikesh

**Location:** Banks of the Ganges, Rishikesh, India
**Period:** 1890, 1897
**Significance:** Himalayan meditation


The 'Yoga capital of the world' on the Ganges. Vivekananda spent time in meditation here, studying with yogis and deepening his Himalayan practice. He returned after coming back from the West.


**From the Complete Works** (14 references found):

> \
> \
> Ghazipur \
> February 11, 1890 \
> \
> \
> I have received your book. In Hrishikesh, Kali [Swami Abhedananda] has had a relapse and is again suffering from \
> what seems to be malaria. Once it comes, the fever does not easily leave those who have never had it before. I too \
> suffered the same way when I first had the attack of fever. Kali has never had the fever before. I have not received \
> any letter from Hrishikesh. Where is ... ? \
> \
> I am suffering terribly from a backache which began in Allahabad. I had recovered from it some time back, but it \
> has recurred. So I will have to stay here awhile longer because of my back and also because Babaji [Pavhari Baba] \

*— Notes of Lectures and Classes \*

> (Translated from Bengali ) \
> \
> I \
> \
> Om Namo Narayanaya, ("Salutations to the Lord"; the usual form of addressing a Sannyasin. These memoirs of his second journey to \
> the West were addressed to Swami Trigunatitananda, Editor, Udbodhan and hence this form of address.) Swami. \'97 Pronounce the last \
> syllable of the second word in a high pitch, brother, in the Hrishikesh fashion. For seven days we have been on \
> board the ship and every day I think of writing to you something about our mode of life, and of writing materials \
> also you have given me enough, but the characteristic lethargy of a Bengali stands in the way and foils everything. \
> In the first place, there is idleness; every day…

> \
> \
> \
> 78. While speaking on the true spirit of a Sannyasin, Swami Vivekananda said: \
> \
> \
> "I saw many great men in Hrishikesh. One case that I remember was that of a man who seemed to be mad. He was \
> coming nude down the street, with boys pursuing and throwing stones at him. The whole man was bubbling over \
> with laughter while blood was streaming down his face and neck. I took him and bathed the wound, putting ashes \
> on it to stop the bleeding. And all the time with peals of laughter he told me of the fun the boys and he had been \
> having, throwing the stones. \'92So the Father plays', he said. \
> \
> "Many of these men hide, in order to guard themselves against intrusion. People are a…

> Ghazipur, \
> 19th Feb., 1890. \
> \
> \
> I wrote a letter to brother Gangadhar asking him to stop his wandering and settle down somewhere and to send me \
> an account of the various Sadhus he had come across in Tibet and their ways and customs. I enclose the reply that \
> came from him. Brother Kali is having repeated attacks of fever at Hrishikesh. I have sent him a wire from this \
> place. So if from the reply I find I am wanted by him, I shall be obliged to start direct for Hrishikesh from this \
> place, otherwise I am coming to you in a day or two. Well, you may smile, sir, to see me weaving all this web of \
> Maya \'97 and that is no doubt the fact. But then there is the chain of iron, and there…

> 2nd April, 1890. \
> \
> \
> Glad to receive your letter as well as Pramada Babu's and Baburam's (Premananda's). I am doing pretty well here. \
> You have expressed a desire to see me. I too have a similar longing, and it is this that makes me afraid of going. \
> Moreover, the Babaji forbids me to do so. I shall try to go on a few days\'92 leave from him. But there is this fear that \
> by so doing I shall be drawn up to the hills by the attraction I have for Hrishikesh, and it will be very difficult to \
> shake it off, specially for one weak-minded, you see, like myself. The attack of lumbago, too, will not leave me on \
> any account \'97 a botheration! But then I am getting used to it. Please convey…


---

### Uttarkashi

**Location:** Upper Ganges valley, Uttarkashi, India
**Period:** 1890
**Significance:** Deep Himalayan retreat


Vivekananda spent extended time in meditation at Uttarkashi in the deep Himalayas. A yogi he met here encouraged him to go to the West and spread Vedanta. He later credited this experience as a turning point.


*No direct reference found in the Complete Works.*


---

### Delhi

**Location:** Old Delhi, Delhi, India
**Period:** 1888–1891
**Significance:** Historical capital


Vivekananda visited Delhi during his wandering years. He explored the historical monuments — the Red Fort, Jama Masjid, Humayun's Tomb — and was struck by the layered civilisational history of India's ancient capital.

### Alwar

**Location:** Alwar Palace, Alwar, India
**Period:** 1891
**Significance:** Royal encounter


Vivekananda visited the court of the Maharaja of Alwar, Mangal Singh, in 1891. In a famous exchange, the Maharaja's contempt for image worship was countered when Vivekananda asked a courtier to spit on a portrait of the Maharaja — demonstrating that God too is present in images.

### Jaipur (Jeypore)

**Location:** Pink City, Jaipur, India
**Period:** 1891
**Significance:** Cultural visit


The Pink City of Rajasthan. Vivekananda visited Jaipur during his wandering tour of Rajputana, observing the vibrant culture and religious practices of the region. He engaged with scholars and learned men at the royal court.


**From the Complete Works** (11 references found):

> \

> to look after the religious needs of the Indian emigrants in Africa. He will of course send the man and bear all \
> expenses. \

> terribly for his master in going to and from Madras and with his eye wholly on the fact that his master would be…

> be a very practical man in time. Tender my loving regards to…

> The 9th July 1897 \
> \
> \
> I haven't received any word of your arrival yet. I heard that Alasinga has gone there with his relations by way of \
> Jaipur. We stayed at the Binsar Dak Bungalow [rest-house] for two or three days, and then I left for Shyamdhura. \
> At this, Miss [Henrietta] Muller got infuriated and left for Almora. Terribly upset, Miss Muller accused Shivananda \

> \
> . . . Leave words when you start for Bombay to somebody to take care of three Sannyasins I am sending to Jaipur. \
> Give them food and good lodging. They will be there till I come. They are fellows \'97 innocent, not learned. They \
> belong to me, and one is my Gurubhai (brother-disciple). If they like, take them to Khetri where I will come soon. \

> humbug which brings no practical good. I must make a silent attempt to start my institution in Calcutta; for that I \
> am going to visit different centres quietly to collect funds. \

### Ajmer (Ajmere)

**Location:** Ajmer Sharif / Pushkar, Ajmer, India
**Period:** 1891
**Significance:** Sufi pilgrimage


Home of the Dargah of Moinuddin Chishti, one of India's most revered Sufi shrines. Vivekananda visited Ajmer as part of his all-India wandering, observing how Islam had taken root in India and reflecting on the universality of spiritual longing.


**From the Complete Works** (4 references found):

> him start a Meerut centre, if he can and, try to have a paper in Hindi. I shall help a little now and then. I shall send \

### Khetri

**Location:** Palace of Khetri, Khetri, India
**Period:** 1891, 1893
**Significance:** Named Vivekananda here


The Maharaja of Khetri, Ajit Singh, became one of Vivekananda's most devoted supporters. It was here, in 1893, that the monk was given the name 'Vivekananda' by the Maharaja before his departure for America. The Maharaja also funded his passage to Chicago.


**From the Complete Works** (77 references found):

> \
> \'ab \
> \
> VEDANTISM \
> \
> At Khetri on 20th December 1897, Swami Vivekananda delivered a lecture on Vedantism in the hall of the \
> Maharaja's bungalow in which he lodged with his disciples. The Swami was introduced by the Raja, who was the \
> president of the meeting; and he spoke for more than an hour and a half. The Swami was at his best, and it was a \
> matter of regret that no shorthand writer was present to report this interesting lecture at length. The following is a \
> summary from notes taken down at the time: \
> \

### Mount Abu

**Location:** Dilwara Jain Temples, Mount Abu, India
**Period:** 1891
**Significance:** Artistic marvel


India's only hill station in Rajasthan. Vivekananda visited the famous Dilwara Jain temples, whose marble carvings moved him to tears. He wrote about the extraordinary artistic achievement as an expression of India's spiritual genius.

> address from a meeting held of Rajput nobility at Mount Abu, for my work in this country. \
> \

---

### Ahmedabad

**Location:** Old City, Ahmedabad, India
**Period:** 1891
**Significance:** Cultural hub


Vivekananda visited Ahmedabad, observing the prosperous trading community and its religious institutions. He studied the interaction of Hindu, Jain and Islamic traditions that had shaped this city's unique culture.


*No direct reference found in the Complete Works.*


---

### Junagadh

**Location:** Girnar Hills, Junagadh, India
**Period:** 1891
**Significance:** Ancient pilgrimage


Vivekananda climbed the Girnar Hills, one of the holiest Jain pilgrimage sites with temples dating back centuries. He spent time in meditation and study here, reflecting on India's ancient spiritual traditions.


**From the Complete Works** (2 references found):

> N. Y. \
> 2nd July 1895 \
> \
> Dear Mother \'97 \
> \
> You did not write to me a single line for a long time. Neither did Sister Mary write about the duty paid on the rugs \
> [from the Dewan of Junagadh]. I am afraid the rugs are small. \
> \
> Here is another consignment from Raja Ajit Singh [the Maharaja of Khetri] consisting of carpets, shawls, etc., etc., \
> for which the bill of lading you sent me the other day. This consignment has no duty to pay because it was all \
> prepaid in India, and the bill of lading says so expressly. I will send you the bill of lading and the receipt for the \
> duty. Kindly take one more trouble for me and get it out of the express company. And keep it with you till I come.…

> \
> [Cambridge , Mass. \
> 21 December 1894 ] \
> \
> Dear Mother, \
> \
> I am glad that Haridas Viharidas (The Dewanof Junagadh.) has sent the rugs. I am afraid they will take a long time to \
> reach here. The Raja (Maharaja Ajit Singh, the Raja of Khetri.) was very much pleased with the phonograph, as he writes, \
> and has heard my voice several times. Hope he will bring it into life. \
> \
> I have not seen Sister Mary yet, but hope to see her this week as I am going away to New York next Tuesday. \
> Cannot come by any means to Chicago now, for I expect to go to Washington from New York and hope to be \
> pretty busy in New York. \

*— lectures on tone building etc. \*

### Dwarka

**Location:** Dwarkadhish Temple, Dwarka, India
**Period:** 1891
**Significance:** Sacred char dham


One of the four sacred dhams (Char Dham) of Hinduism, associated with Lord Krishna. Vivekananda visited Dwarka as part of his pan-India pilgrimage, deeply connecting with the Vaishnava tradition at this ancient coastal shrine.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references found):

> from brother Gangadhar. He is now in Rambag Samadhi, Srinagar, Kashmir. I am greatly suffering from lumbago. \
> \
> Yours etc., \
> \
> VlVEKANANDA. \
> \
> PS. Rakhal and Subodh have come to Vrindaban after visiting Omkar, Gimar, Abu, Bombay, and Dwarka. \
> \

> \

### Porbandar

**Location:** Porbandar, Porbandar, India
**Period:** 1891
**Significance:** Coastal visit


The coastal city of Porbandar (later birthplace of Mahatma Gandhi). Vivekananda stayed here as a wandering monk, engaging with local scholars and experiencing the vibrant religious culture of the Saurashtra coast.


*No direct reference found in the Complete Works.*


---

### Vadodara (Baroda)

**Location:** Laxmi Vilas Palace area, Vadodara, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** Royal visit


Vivekananda visited the progressive court of the Gaekwad of Baroda, a state known for reformist policies and support of education. He engaged with the intelligentsia and observed India's capacity for modernisation while preserving spiritual values.


**From the Complete Works** (6 references found):

> Lord. \
> \
> Recently my health was very bad, and so I have been delayed, otherwise I had intended to leave for the Punjab this \
> week. The doctor had advised me not to go to the plains at the present time, as it is very hot there. Perhaps I may \
> reach Karachi by about the last week of October. Now I am doing somewhat well. There is nobody else with me \
> now excepting two American friends \'97 ladies. Probably I shall part from them at Lahore. They will wait for me in \
> Calcutta or in Rajputana. I shall probably visit Cutch, Bhuj, Junagad, Bhavnagar, Limbdi, and Baroda and then \
> proceed to Calcutta. My present plan is to go to America via China and Japan in November or December, but it is…

> 15th May, 1901. \
> \
> \
> Your letter from Naini Tal is quite exciting. I have just returned from my tour through East Bengal and Assam. As \
> usual I am quite tired and broken down. \
> \
> If some real good comes out of a visit to H. H. of Baroda I am ready to come over, otherwise I don't want to \
> undergo the expense and exertion of the long journey Think it well over and make inquiries, and write me if you \
> still thi nk it would be best for the Cause for me to come to see H. H. . . . \
> \
> \
> Yours with love and blessings, \
> \

> VI \
> \
> \
> Dear DlWANJI SAHEB, (Shri Haridas Viharidas Desai) \
> \
> \
> Baroda, \
> 26th April, 1892. \
> \
> \
> Very happy to receive your kind letter even here. I had not the least difficulty in reaching your house from the \
> station of Nadiad. And your brothers, they are what they should be, your brothers. May the Lord shower his \
> choicest blessings on your family. I have never found such a glorious one in all my travels. Your friend Mr. \

> DEAR Joe, (Miss Josephine MacLeod.) \
> \
> Just now received your nice long letter. I am so glad that you met and approve Miss Cornelia Sorabji. I knew her \
> father at Poona, also a younger sister who was in America. Perhaps her mother will remember me as the Sannyasin \
> who used to live with the Thakore Sahib of Limbdi at Poona. \
> \
> I hope you will go to Baroda and see the Maharani. \
> \
> I am much better and hope to continue so for some time. I have just now a beautiful letter from Mrs. Sevier in \
> which she writes a whole lot of beautiful things about you. \
> \
> I am so glad you saw Mr. Tata and find him so strong and good. \

### Mumbai (Bombay)

**Location:** Bombay, various locations, Mumbai, India
**Period:** 1892, 1893, 1897
**Significance:** Gateway to the world


India's commercial capital. Vivekananda passed through Bombay multiple times — on his way to the Parliament of Religions (1893) he embarked from here. On his return (1897) he received a grand reception. He also visited the Kanheri Caves and observed the modern city's remarkable energy.

> "Thou art He that beareth the burdens of the universe; help me to bear the little burden of this life." (ND 2: 32) \

### Pune (Poona)

**Location:** Pune, Pune, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** Meeting with scholars


Pune was the intellectual centre of Maharashtra. Vivekananda visited and met Bal Gangadhar Tilak and other reformers. He was deeply impressed by the political and social awakening he observed here.


**From the Complete Works** (7 references found):

> \
> One great prophet, however, arose in the north, Govind Singh, the last Guru of the Sikhs, with creative genius; and \
> the result of his spiritual work was followed by the well-known political organisation of the Sikhs. We have seen \
> throughout the history of India, a spirtitual upheaval is almost always succeeded by a political unity extending over \
> more or less area of the continent, which in its turn helps to strengthen the spiritual aspiration that brings it to \
> being. But the spiritual aspiration that preceded the rise of the Mahratta or the Sikh empire was entirely reactionary. \
> We seek in vain to find in the court of Poona or Lahore even a ray of reflection of that intellectual…

> they persecuted me all the time in America, because I did not join them. They wanted to begin it here. So I had to \
> clear my position. If that displeases any of my Calcutta friends, "God help them". You need not be afraid, I do not \
> work alone, but He is always with me. What could I do otherwise? \

> Never have I laid a curse on you, and why should I do so now? All your life you have known my love for you, and \
> today are you doubting it? True, my temper was ever bad, and nowadays owing to illness it occasionally becomes \
> terrible \'97 but know this for certain that my love can never cease. \
> \
> My health nowadays is becoming a little better. Have the rains started in Madras? When the rains begin a little in \
> the South, I may go to Madras via Bombay and Poona. With the onset of the rains the terrible heat of the South \
> will perhaps subside. \
> \
> My great love to you and all others. Yesterday Sharat returned to the Math from Darjeeling \'97 his health is much \
> better than it was…

### Belgaum (Belagavi)

**Location:** Belgaum, Belagavi, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** Transition point


Vivekananda passed through Belgaum on his journey from Maharashtra to Karnataka. He engaged with locals and scholars, observing the transition in language, culture and religious practice as he moved south.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references found):

> \
> Bombay, \
> 24th May, 1893. \
> \
> \
> Very glad to receive your letter and that of dear Haripada. Please do not be sorry that I could not write to you very \
> often. I am always praying to the Lord for your welfare. I cannot go to Belgaum now as arrangements are all ready \
> for my starting for America on the 31st next. The Lord willing, I shall see you on returning from my travels in \
> America and Europe. Always resign yourselves to the Lord Shri Krishna. Always remember that we are but \
> puppets in the Lord's hands. Remain pure always. Please be careful not to become impure even in thought, as also \
> in speech and action; always try to do good to others as far as in you lies. And remember that…

### Bengaluru (Bangalore)

**Location:** Palace area / Cubbon Park, Bengaluru, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** Meeting Diwan


Vivekananda visited the Mysore Diwan (Prime Minister) Sheshadri Iyer in Bangalore, who was deeply impressed by the monk's vision and arranged his introduction to the Maharaja of Mysore. This visit was crucial in securing support for his voyage to America.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references found):

> Dear Alasinga, \
> \
> Your friend, the young graduate, came to receive me at the station, so also a Bengali gentleman. At present I am \
> living with the Bengali gentleman; tomorrow I go to live with your young friend for a few days, and then I see the \
> different sights here, and in a few days you may expect me at Madras. For I am very sorry to tell you that I cannot \
> go back at present to Rajputana. It is so very dreadfully hot here already. I do not know how hot it would be at \
> Rajputana, and I cannot bear heat at all. So the next thing, I would do, would be to go back to Bangalore and then \
> to Ootacamund to pass the summer there. My brain boils in heat. \
> \
> So all my plans have been…

### Mysuru (Mysore)

**Location:** Mysore Palace, Mysore, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** Royal patronage


Vivekananda had a remarkable audience with the Maharaja of Mysore, Chamarajendra Wadiyar. The Maharaja reportedly offered to fund his voyage to America — though Vivekananda ultimately felt he must go without royal support, trusting in divine will.


**From the Complete Works** (22 references found):

> there and began our talk with an expression of surprise at his assent to my request. \

### Hyderabad

**Location:** Hyderabad city, Hyderabad, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** Cross-cultural encounter


Vivekananda visited Hyderabad, the seat of the Nizam — one of India's wealthiest Muslim rulers. He observed the unique cultural synthesis of Hindu and Islamic traditions in this cosmopolitan city and engaged with scholars of both traditions.


**From the Complete Works** (5 references found):

> 15th June, 1892. \
> \
> \
> It is a long time since I heard from you. I hope I have not offended you anyway. I came down with the Thakore \
> Saheb of Mahabaleshwar, and I am living here with him. I would remain here a week or more and then proceed to \
> Rameshwaram via Hyderabad. \
> \
> Perhaps by this time every hitch has been removed from your way in Junagad; at least I hope so. I am very anxious \
> to leam about your health, especially that sprain, you know. \
> \
> I saw your friend the Surti tutor to the Prince of Bhavnagar. He is a perfect gentleman. It was quite a privilege to \
> make his acquaintance; he is so good and noble-natured a man. \

> \
> Another of us was a physician. He thought of a sentence from a German medical book. It was written on his paper. \
> \
> Several days later I went to this man again, thinking possibly I had been deluded somehow before. I took other \
> friends, and on this occasion also he came out wonderfully triumphant. \
> \
> Another time I was in the city of Hyderabad in India, and I was told of a Brahmin there who could produce \
> numbers of things from where, nobody knew. This man was in business there; he was a respectable gentleman. \
> \
> And I asked him to show me his tricks. It so happened that this man had a fever, and in India there is a general \
> belief that if a holy man puts his hand on a sick man he…

### Madras (Chennai)

**Location:** Triplicane / Castle Kernan, Chennai, India
**Period:** 1892–1893, 1897
**Significance:** Launched for America from here


Madras (now Chennai) was pivotal in Vivekananda's life. The young disciple Alasinga Perumal and a group of enthusiastic supporters urged him to attend the Parliament. They raised funds and spread his message. On his return in 1897, Madras gave him a triumphant welcome with a massive public reception.


**From the Complete Works** (159 references found):

> Shri Ramakrishna has mercifully made us understand the evil of these powers as being \
> hindrances to real spirituality that we are able to take them at their proper value. Haven\'92t \
> you noticed how for that reason the children of Shri Ramakrishna pay no heed to them? \
> \

> Bangal (Lit. A man from East Bengal, i.e. the disciple.) that incident of yours in Madras when you \
> met the famous ghost-tamer?" \
> \

> whose very soul, as it were, is in religion. I have seen a little of the world, travelling among the races of the East \
> and the West; and everywhere I find among nations one great ideal which forms the backbone, so to speak, of that \

> backbone, for the bed-rock upon which the whole building of its life has been based. Some of you may…

> America without any introductions or friends there, I called on the leader of the Theosophical Society. Naturally I \

> reached America, as you know, through the help of a few friends of Madras. Most of them are present here. Only \

### Kanyakumari (Cape Comorin)

**Location:** Vivekananda Rock Memorial, Kanyakumari, India
**Period:** 24–26 December 1892
**Significance:** Decisive moment — birth of his world mission


On 24 December 1892, Vivekananda swam to the rock at the southernmost tip of India and meditated for three days. Here, sitting where three seas meet, he had a vision that crystallised his life's mission: to uplift India and carry India's spiritual wisdom to the world. This was the turning point of his life.


**From the Complete Works** (8 references found):

> Every moment heard the utterance of wisdom gathered superconsciously. Everyone about him caught the vision of \
> the divine. Upon the disciple came the desire for supreme knowledge "as if it had been a fever". Yet he who was \
> thus the living embodiment of the books was so unconsciously, for he had read none of them! In his Guru, \
> Ramakrishna Paramahamsa, Vivekananda found the key to life. \
> \
> Even now, however, the preparation for his own task was not complete. He had yet to wander throughout the length \
> and breadth of India, from the Himalayas to Cape Comorin, mixing with saints and scholars and simple souls alike, \
> learning from all, teaching to all, and living with all, seeing India…

> Everyone about him caught the vision of the divine. Upon the disciple came the desire for \
> supreme knowledge "as if it had been a fever". Yet he who was thus the living \
> embodiment of the books was so unconsciously, for he had read none of them! In his \
> Guru, Ramakrishna Paramahamsa, Vivekananda found the key to life. \
> \
> Even now, however, the preparation for his own task was not complete. He had yet to \
> wander throughout the length and breadth of India, from the Himalayas to Cape Comorin, \
> mixing with saints and scholars and simple souls alike, learning from all, teaching to all, \
> and living with all, seeing India as she was and is, and so grasping in its \
> comprehensiveness that…

> between us. I have got a hundred men and women around me; if I become the devil himself tomorrow, they will \
> say, "Here we are still! We will never give you up!" That is a great blessing. In happiness, in misery, in famine, in \
> pain, in the grave, in heaven, or in hell who never gives me up is my friend. Is such friendship a joke? A man may \
> have salvation through such friendship. That brings salvation if we can love like that. If we have that faithfulness, \

> India will be raised, not with the power of the flesh, but with the power of the spirit; not with the flag of \

> \
> So every improvement in India requires first of all an upheaval in religion. Before flooding India with socialistic or \
> political ideas, first deluge the land with spiritual ideas. The first work that demands our attention is that the most \
> wonderful truths confined in our Upanishads, in our scriptures, in our Puranas must be brought out from the books, \
> brought out from the monasteries, brought out from the forests, brought out from the possession of selected bodies \
> of people, and scattered broadcast all over the land, so that these truths may run like fire all over the country from \
> north to south and east to west, from the Himalayas to Comorin, from Sindh to the Brahmaputra.…

### Rameswaram

**Location:** Ramanathaswamy Temple, Rameswaram, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** Sacred pilgrimage


One of the Char Dham pilgrimage sites, associated with Lord Rama. Vivekananda visited the magnificent Ramanathaswamy Temple and was welcomed by the local Raja (Bhaskara Setupati of Ramnad) who became his enthusiastic patron and sponsor.


**From the Complete Works** (8 references found):

> His Most Holiness, \
> \
> Sri Paramahamsa, Yati-Raja, Digvijaya-Kolahala, Sarvamata-Sampratipanna, Parama-Yogeeswara, Srimat \
> Bhagavan Sree Ramakrishna Paramahamsa Karakamala Sanjata, Rajadhiraja-Sevita, SREE VIVEKANANDA SWAMI, \
> May It Please Your Holiness, \
> \
> We, the inhabitants of this ancient and historic Samsthanam of Sethu Bandha Rameswaram, otherwise known as \
> Ramanathapuram or Ramnad, beg, most cordially, to welcome you to this, our motherland. We deem it a very rare \
> privilege to be the first to pay your Holiness our heartfelt homage on your landing in India, and that, on the shores \
> sanctified by the footsteps of that great Hero and our revered Lord \'97 Sree Bhagavan…

> MY DEAR SHASHI, (Swami Ramakrishnananda) \

> anybody. I have neither the strength nor the money, nor the will to take up anybody with me. Bhaktas (devotees) of \
> Guru Maharaj or not, it does not matter. It was very foolish of you even to ask such a question. Let me tell you \
> again, I am more dead than alive, and strictly refuse to see anybody. If you cannot manage this, I don't go to \
> Madras. I have to become a bit selfish to save my…

The Raja of Ramnad (Bhaskara Setupati) was one of Vivekananda's earliest and most fervent supporters. He urged the Swami to attend the Parliament of World Religions and helped raise funds for his passage. Vivekananda was the Raja's guest of honour.

> India and started a little monastery in Calcutta. During my travels, I came to Madras, where I received help from \
> the Maharaja of Mysore and the Raja of Ramnad." \

### Madurai (Madura)

**Location:** Meenakshi Amman Temple, Madurai, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** Temple city


Vivekananda visited the magnificent Meenakshi Temple in Madurai, moved by the breathtaking gopurams and the living faith of the devotees. He reflected on India's capacity to maintain spiritual culture across millennia.

### Trivananthapuram (Travancore)

**Location:** Padmanabhaswamy Temple area, Thiruvananthapuram, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** Southern extremity


Vivekananda visited Trivandrum, home of the famous Padmanabhaswamy Temple (Lord Vishnu). He had audiences with scholars and the royal family. Kerala's high literacy and unique matrilineal customs deeply interested him.

### Puri (Jagannath Temple)

**Location:** Jagannath Temple, Puri, India
**Period:** 1888
**Significance:** Sacred dham


One of the four sacred Char Dhams of Hinduism, home of Lord Jagannath. Vivekananda visited Puri during his wandering years, deeply moved by the all-embracing Jagannath tradition that transcended caste — serving prasad to all, regardless of social status.

**Location:** 16, Bosepara Lane, Simulia, Kolkata, India
**Period:** 1877–1884
**Significance:** Childhood home


The family residence at 16 Bosepara Lane, Simulia, Kolkata, where Narendranath Datta grew up. The family moved here from Gourmohan Mukherjee Street. Vivekananda spent his formative teenage years at this address.

Barajaguli in Nadia district was visited during Vivekananda's wandering pilgrimage. The Nadia district, birthplace of Sri Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, held deep spiritual significance for him.

> 26th April, 1892. \
> \
> \
> Very happy to receive your kind letter even here. I had not the least difficulty in reaching your house from the \
> station of Nadiad. And your brothers, they are what they should be, your brothers. May the Lord shower his \
> choicest blessings on your family. I have never found such a glorious one in all my travels. Your friend Mr. \
> Manibhai has provided every comfort for me; but, as to his company, I have only seen him twice; once for a \
> minute, the other for ten minutes at the most when he talked about the system of education here. Of course, I have \
> seen the Library and the pictures of Ravi Varma, and that is about all worth seeing here. So I am…

> would soon enter a learned profession which might bring him respect, fame, and, above all, a good salary and a \
> high position. But then there was this Sanskrit! The vast majority of European scholars had not even heard of it \
> then; as for making it pay \'97 I have already said that such a thing is impossible even now. Yet his desire to leam it \
> was strong. \
> \
> It has unfortunately become hard for us modem Indians to understand how it could be like that; nevertheless, there \
> are to be met with in Varanasi and Nadia and other places even now, some old as well as young persons among our \
> Pandits, and mostly among the Sannyasins, who are mad with this kind of thirst for knowledge for its…


---

### Kuppaghat, Bhagalpur

**Location:** Kuppaghat Ghat, Bhagalpur, India
**Period:** 1888
**Significance:** Tapasya on Ganga banks


Kuppaghat on the banks of the Ganga at Bhagalpur is where Vivekananda stayed and practiced deep meditation (tapasya) during his parivrajaka (wandering monk) years. He resided in a small ashrama here for some time.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references found):

> Through the Divine Mother's will, they will be provided for. When you get a horse, never you worry about the \
> whip. ... Get together whomsoever you can lay your hands on, no picking and choosing now \'97 everything will be \
> set right in course of time. In every attempt there are many obstacles to cope with, but gradually the path becomes \
> smooth. \
> \
> Convey to the European officer many thanks from me. Work on fearlessly \'97 there is a hero! Bravo! Thrice well \
> done! The starting of a centre at Bhagalpur that you have written about is no doubt a good idea \'97 enlightening the \
> schoolboys and things of that sort. But our mission is for the destitute, the poor, and the illiterate…

*— notes of praise. But then the machine has already been set agoing, and even a boy can work it, or a fool add to it \*

### Bhagalpur

**Location:** Bhagalpur, Bhagalpur, India
**Period:** 1888
**Significance:** Wandering pilgrimage stop


Bhagalpur on the Ganga was an important stop during Vivekananda's wandering years. The ancient silk city has strong ties to the Ganga ghats where ascetics traditionally gathered.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references found):

### Tehri

**Location:** Old Tehri, Tehri, India
**Period:** 1890
**Significance:** Wandering stop, met Raja of Tehri


Vivekananda visited the princely state of Tehri Garhwal during his Himalayan wanderings. He met the Raja of Tehri and spent time meditating in this Garhwal hill region on the banks of the Bhagirathi river.


**From the Complete Works** (2 references found):

> five yards below the surface \'97 that wonderfully sweet, ice-cold "charming water of the Ganga (From Valmiki's \
> hymn.)", and that wonderful sound of "Hara, Hara" of the running water, and the echo of "Hara, Hara" from the \
> neighbouring mountain-falls? Do you remember that life in the forest, the begging of Madhukari (Meaning, collected \
> from door to door, in small bits.) alms, eating on small islands of rock in the bed of the Ganga, hearty drinking of that \
> water with the palms, and the fearless wandering of fishes all round for crumbs of bread? You remember that love \
> for Ganga water, that glory of the Ganga, the touch of its water that makes the mind dispassionate, that Ganga…

> \
> \
> I got all news about you from Hariprasanna. I am especially pleased to hear that Rakhal and Hari are now quite \
> well. \
> \
> \
> Now Babu Raghunath Bhattacharya of Tehri is suffering very much from some pain in the neck; I also have been \
> suffering for a long time from some pain at the back of my neck. If you can get hold of some very old ghee, then \
> send some of it to him at Dehra Dun and some of it to me also at my Khetri address. You are sure to get it from \
> Habu or Sharat (lawyer). Address it to Babu Raghunath Bhattacharya, Dehra Dun, N.W.P. . . . and it will reach \
> him. \
> \
> The day after tomorrow I am leaving for Saharanpur; from there to Rajputana. \

### Jodhpur

**Location:** Jodhpur city, Jodhpur, India
**Period:** 1891
**Significance:** Royal patronage


Vivekananda visited Jodhpur during his wandering years in Rajputana. He spent time with the Maharaja of Jodhpur who was greatly impressed by the wandering monk. The fortified Blue City left a deep impression on Vivekananda.


**From the Complete Works** (2 references found):

> In case Gupta [Swami Sadananda] has lost his mental balance, ask him to come to Almora \'97 but only when the \
> boy selected for Shashi reaches there. I received a letter from R. A. [Rajam Aiyer?]. The money he sent has \
> reached the Math. I have received two volumes of Ramanuja's commentary. Advise him to send me the third. Ask \
> G. G. [Narasimhachari] to send me similar commentaries by Madhva and others, if he can. \
> \
> A public meeting will have to be organized at Madras to present an address of welcome to the Raja [Ajit Singh] of \
> Khetri and to Pratap Singh of Jodhpur for their boldness in visiting England as well as for representing their \
> principalities in India in the Jubilee…

> \
> CXVI \
> \
> To Sister Christine \
> \
> \
> Jodhpur, Rajputana, \
> 4th January 1898. \
> \
> Love and greetings etc. to thee, dear Christina, and a happy New Year. May it find you younger in heart, stronger \
> in body, and purer in spirit. \
> \
> I am still travelling in season and out of season. Lecturing some, working a good deal. \

### Wadhwan

**Period:** 1891
**Significance:** Royal hospitality


Wadhwan, a princely state in Saurashtra, was visited by Vivekananda during his Kathiawar journey. The local ruler offered hospitality to the wandering monk as he traversed the sacred landscape of Gujarat.


*No direct reference found in the Complete Works.*

### Limbdi

**Location:** Limbdi, Limbdi, India
**Period:** 1891
**Significance:** Royal stay


Limbdi, a small princely state in Saurashtra, was visited by Vivekananda. The Thakore Sahib of Limbdi was among the Rajput rulers who welcomed and supported the wandering monk during his extended tour of Gujarat.


**From the Complete Works** (4 references found):

> Lord. \
> \
> Recently my health was very bad, and so I have been delayed, otherwise I had intended to leave for the Punjab this \
> week. The doctor had advised me not to go to the plains at the present time, as it is very hot there. Perhaps I may \
> reach Karachi by about the last week of October. Now I am doing somewhat well. There is nobody else with me \
> now excepting two American friends \'97 ladies. Probably I shall part from them at Lahore. They will wait for me in \
> Calcutta or in Rajputana. I shall probably visit Cutch, Bhuj, Junagad, Bhavnagar, Limbdi, and Baroda and then \
> proceed to Calcutta. My present plan is to go to America via China and Japan in November or December, but it is…

> Khetri, \
> 14th December, 1897. \
> \
> \
> I have today sent your power of attorney with my signature. . . . Draw the money as early as you can, and wire to \
> me as soon as you have done so. A Raja of a place in Bundelkhand named Chatrapur has invited me. I shall visit \
> the place on my way to the Math. The Raja of Limbdi, too, is writing earnestly. I cannot avoid going there also. I \
> shall make a lightning tour of Kathiawar \'97 that is what it will come to. I shall feel great relief as soon as I reach \
> Calcutta. . . . There is no news from Boston as yet; perhaps Sharat is coming; anyway, whenever any news comes \
> from anywhere, write to me immediately. \
> \
> \
> Yours affectionately, \

> 17th February, 1901. \
> \
> DEAR Joe, (Miss Josephine MacLeod.) \
> \
> Just now received your nice long letter. I am so glad that you met and approve Miss Cornelia Sorabji. I knew her \
> father at Poona, also a younger sister who was in America. Perhaps her mother will remember me as the Sannyasin \
> who used to live with the Thakore Sahib of Limbdi at Poona. \
> \
> I hope you will go to Baroda and see the Maharani. \
> \
> I am much better and hope to continue so for some time. I have just now a beautiful letter from Mrs. Sevier in \
> which she writes a whole lot of beautiful things about you. \
> \

> \
> \'ab Chronology \
> \
> XIX \
> \
> \
> Dear Vehemia Chand Limbdi, \
> \
> \
> Washington, \
> 23rd October, 1894. \
> \
> \

### Girnar

**Location:** Girnar, Junagadh, India
**Period:** 1891
**Significance:** Ancient sacred mountain


Girnar, the sacred mountain near Junagadh revered by Hindus, Jains, and Buddhists alike, was visited by Vivekananda. He climbed this ancient hill of temples and meditated at its sacred sites, communing with the divine in its ancient sanctuaries.


**From the Complete Works** (3 references found):

> N. Y. \
> 2nd July 1895 \
> \
> Dear Mother \'97 \
> \
> You did not write to me a single line for a long time. Neither did Sister Mary write about the duty paid on the rugs \
> [from the Dewan of Junagadh]. I am afraid the rugs are small. \
> \
> Here is another consignment from Raja Ajit Singh [the Maharaja of Khetri] consisting of carpets, shawls, etc., etc., \
> for which the bill of lading you sent me the other day. This consignment has no duty to pay because it was all \
> prepaid in India, and the bill of lading says so expressly. I will send you the bill of lading and the receipt for the \
> duty. Kindly take one more trouble for me and get it out of the express company. And keep it with you till I come.…

> All these considerations may have had weight with our young Brahmacharin, but we are sure that the chief among \
> them was the thirst for knowledge. Of his travels we kn ow but little, except that, from his knowledge of Dravidian \
> languages, in which a good deal of the literature of his sect is written, and his thorough acquaintance with the old \
> Bengali of the Vaishnavas of Shri Chaitanya's order, we infer that his stay in Southern India and Bengal could not \
> have been very short. \
> \
> But on his visit to one place, the friends of his youth lay great stress. It was on the top of mount Girnar in \
> Kathiawar, they say, that he was first initiated into the mysteries of practical Yoga. \
> \
> It…

> \
> [Cambridge , Mass. \
> 21 December 1894 ] \
> \
> Dear Mother, \
> \
> I am glad that Haridas Viharidas (The Dewanof Junagadh.) has sent the rugs. I am afraid they will take a long time to \
> reach here. The Raja (Maharaja Ajit Singh, the Raja of Khetri.) was very much pleased with the phonograph, as he writes, \
> and has heard my voice several times. Hope he will bring it into life. \
> \
> I have not seen Sister Mary yet, but hope to see her this week as I am going away to New York next Tuesday. \
> Cannot come by any means to Chicago now, for I expect to go to Washington from New York and hope to be \
> pretty busy in New York. \

*— lectures on tone building etc. \*

**Location:** Bhuj, Kutch, Bhuj, India
**Period:** 1891
**Significance:** Kutch wandering


Vivekananda visited Bhuj, the capital of the Kutch region, during his Gujarat wanderings. The arid landscape of Kutch, with its unique culture and proximity to the Rann, offered a stark spiritual backdrop for the wandering monk.

### Veraval

**Location:** Veraval port, Veraval, India
**Period:** 1891
**Significance:** Somnath pilgrimage gateway


Veraval was visited by Vivekananda as the gateway to the sacred Somnath temple. The ancient port city on the Saurashtra coast has deep historical significance as the site of repeated destruction and reconstruction of the Somnath Jyotirlinga.


*No direct reference found in the Complete Works.*

### Narayan Sarovar

**Location:** Koteshwar, Kutch, Koteshwar, India
**Period:** 1891
**Significance:** Sacred pilgrimage lake


Narayan Sarovar, one of the five most sacred lakes (panch sarovars) in Hinduism, was visited by Vivekananda during his Kutch wanderings. The remote sacred lake near the tip of Kutch is a site of immense religious importance.


*No direct reference found in the Complete Works.*

### Mandvi

**Location:** Mandvi port, Kutch, Mandvi, India
**Period:** 1891
**Significance:** Port town visited during Kutch journey


Mandvi, the historic port city of Kutch, was visited by Vivekananda during his extensive Gujarat tour. The town is known for its shipbuilding tradition and the beautiful Vijay Vilas Palace overlooking the sea.

### Beyt Dwarka

**Location:** Beyt Dwarka Island, Okha, India
**Period:** 1891
**Significance:** Sacred island of Lord Krishna


Beyt Dwarka (Bet Dwarka), the sacred island off the coast of Gujarat believed to be the actual residence of Lord Krishna, was visited by Vivekananda during his Saurashtra pilgrimage. The island is considered the actual Dwarka of Krishna's time.


*No direct reference found in the Complete Works.*

### Palitana

**Location:** Shatrunjaya Hills, Palitana, Palitana, India
**Period:** 1891
**Significance:** Jain pilgrimage mountain


Palitana, the foremost Jain pilgrimage destination with 900+ temples on Shatrunjaya Hill, was visited by Vivekananda. He was deeply moved by the Jain tradition of ahimsa and the magnificent cluster of marble temples atop the sacred mountain.


*No direct reference found in the Complete Works.*

### Khandwa

**Location:** Khandwa, Khandwa, India
**Period:** 1890
**Significance:** Wandering stop in central India


Khandwa in central India was visited by Vivekananda during his parivrajaka (wandering monk) years. He traveled extensively through the Narmada valley region of present-day Madhya Pradesh.


**From the Complete Works** (2 references found):

> \
> 1892 \
> \
> Dear DlWANJI SAHEB, (Shri Haridas Viharidas Desai) \
> \
> The bearer of this letter, Babu Akshaya Kumar Ghose, is a particular friend of mine. He comes of a respectable \
> family of Calcutta. I found him at Khandwa where I made his acquaintance, although I knew his family long before \
> in Calcutta. \
> \
> He is a very honest and intelligent boy and is an undergraduate of the Calcutta University. You know how hard the \
> struggle is in Bengal nowadays, and the poor boy has been out in search of some job. Knowing your native \
> kindness of heart, I think I am not disturbing you by asking and entreating you to do something for this young man. \
> \

> \
> \
> Our congratulations for the successful carrying out of the Mahotsava (Big celebration of Shri Ramakrishna's birthday.) in \
> Madras. Hope you had a good gathering and plenty of spiritual food. We are all so glad that you have girded \
> yourself to teach more of spirituality to the Madras people than those finger twistings and klingphat (Cryptic Mantras \
> or sound formulae.) you are so fond of. Really your lecture on Shriji (Shri Ramakrishna.) was splendid. I could only catch \
> a report in the Madras Mail in Khandwa, and the Math people have not had any. Why don't you send us over a \
> copy? \
> \
> I leam that you complain about my silence, is it? I have written you more letters, however, than…

**Significance:** Sacred city of Mahakaleshwar


Ujjain, one of the seven sacred cities of Hinduism and home to the Mahakaleshwar Jyotirlinga, was visited by Vivekananda during his wandering years. He was deeply moved by this ancient center of learning and spirituality.

> And to the south lie the deserts of Arabia \'97 the mother of heroes. Have you ever seen a Bedouin Arab, with a \
> cloak on, and a big kerchief tied on his head with a bunch of woollen strings? \'97 That gait, that pose of standing, \
> and that look, you will find in no other country. From head to foot emanates the freedom of open unconfined desert \
> air \'97 there you have the Arab. When the bigotry of the Christians and the barbarity of the Goths extinguished the \
> ancient Greek and Roman civilisation, when Persia was trying to hide her internal putrefaction by adding layer \
> after layer of gold-leaf upon it, when, in India, the sun of splendour of Pataliputra and Ujjain had set, leaving…

### Maheshwar

**Location:** Maheshwar fort, Narmada, Maheshwar, India
**Period:** 1890

Maheshwar, the historic riverside town on the Narmada known as the domain of Queen Ahilyabai Holkar, was visited by Vivekananda. He revered the Narmada as a sacred river and spent time at the ancient ghats and temples.


*No direct reference found in the Complete Works.*

### Mahabaleshwar

**Location:** Mahabaleshwar hill station, Mahabaleshwar, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** Hill station rest


Mahabaleshwar, the famous hill station in the Sahyadri mountains, was visited by Vivekananda during his Maharashtra travels. The sacred origin of the Krishna river at Mahabaleshwar temple was of particular spiritual interest.


**From the Complete Works** (3 references found):

> Margaon, \
> \
> 1893. \
> \
> \
> I just now received a letter from you. I reached here safe. I went to visit Panjim and a few other villages and \
> temples near by. I returned just today. I have not given up the intention of visiting Gokarna, Mahabaleshwar, and \
> other places. I start for Dharwar by the morning train tomorrow. I have taken the walking-stick with me. Doctor \
> Yagdekar's friend was very hospitable to me. Please give my compliments to Mr. Bhate and all others there. May \
> the Lord shower His blessings on you and your wife. The town of Panjim is very neat and clean. Most of the \
> Christians here are literate. The Hindus are mostly uneducated. \
> \
> \

> \
> Neutral Line, Poona, \
> 15th June, 1892. \
> \
> \
> It is a long time since I heard from you. I hope I have not offended you anyway. I came down with the Thakore \
> Saheb of Mahabaleshwar, and I am living here with him. I would remain here a week or more and then proceed to \
> Rameshwaram via Hyderabad. \
> \
> Perhaps by this time every hitch has been removed from your way in Junagad; at least I hope so. I am very anxious \
> to leam about your health, especially that sprain, you know. \
> \
> I saw your friend the Surti tutor to the Prince of Bhavnagar. He is a perfect gentleman. It was quite a privilege to \

> I am very much gratified on receiving your letter, especially as that is the proof that you have the same kindness \
> towards me. \
> \
> \
> About the kindness and gentlemanliness of your friend Mr. Bederkar of Indore and of the Dakshinis in general, the \
> less said the better; but of course there are Dakshinis and Dakshinis, and I would only quote to you what Shankar \
> Pandurang wrote me at Mahabaleshwar on my informing him that I had found shelter with the Limdi Thakore: \
> \
> "I am so glad to learn that you have found Limdi Thakore there, else you would have been in serious troubles, our \
> Maratha people not being so kind as the Gujaratis." So kind? heaven and hell! \
> \
> I am very glad that your…

Kolhapur, home to the Mahalakshmi temple (one of the Shakti Peethas), was visited by Vivekananda during his South India journey. He received warm hospitality and gave discourses to eager audiences in this royal city.


*No direct reference found in the Complete Works.*

### Nagpur

**Location:** Nagpur city, Nagpur, India
**Period:** 1891
**Significance:** Wandering in central India


Nagpur, the orange city and geographical heart of India, was visited by Vivekananda during his parivrajaka years as he traversed central and southern India. He met local scholars and gave teachings to devotees here.


*No direct reference found in the Complete Works.*


---

### Thrissur (Trichur)

**Location:** Thrissur, Thrissur, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** Cultural capital of Kerala


Thrissur, the cultural capital of Kerala, was visited by Vivekananda during his South India pilgrimage. He interacted with scholars and spiritual leaders in this center of arts, temples, and Vedic tradition.


*No direct reference found in the Complete Works.*


---

### Kodungalloor (Cranganore)

**Location:** Kodungalloor, Kodungalloor, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** Ancient sacred site


Kodungalloor (ancient Cranganore), one of the oldest towns in India with connections to the earliest Jewish, Christian, and Muslim communities in India, was visited by Vivekananda. The Bhagavati temple here is among Kerala's most revered shrines.


*No direct reference found in the Complete Works.*

### Paramakudi

**Location:** Paramakudi, Paramakudi, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** South India tour stop


Paramakudi in the Ramanathapuram district was a stop during Vivekananda's South India journey. This town in the Pandya Nadu region was one of many stops as he made his way through the Tamil heartland.


**From the Complete Works** (2 references found):

> \
> Home / Complete-Works / Volume 3 / Lectures from Colombo to Almora / \
> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> REPLY TO THE ADDRESS OF WELCOME AT PARAMAKUDI \
> \
> Paramakudi was the first stopping-place after leaving Ramnad, and there was a demonstration on a large scale, \
> including the presentation of the following address: \
> \
> \
> SREEMAT VIVEKANANDA SWAMI \

> \

*— Lectures from Colombo to Almora \*


---

### Manamadurai

**Location:** Manamadurai, Manamadurai, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** South India pilgrimage


Manamadurai in Sivaganga district, Tamil Nadu, was visited by Vivekananda during his South Indian wanderings. The town has an ancient Shiva temple and was part of the sacred landscape he traversed en route to Kanyakumari.


*No direct reference found in the Complete Works.*


---

### Kumbakonam

**Location:** Kumbakonam, Kumbakonam, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** City of temples


Kumbakonam, renowned as the 'City of Temples' in the Cauvery delta, was visited by Vivekananda during his South India tour. The town is famous for the Mahamaham tank and dozens of ancient temples, providing a rich spiritual experience.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references found):

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> THE MISSION OF THE VEDANTA \
> \

> Hindu community: \
> \
> \

> \

---

### Pondicherry

**Location:** Pondicherry (Puducherry), Puducherry, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** South India coastal visit


Vivekananda visited Pondicherry, the French colonial enclave on the Coromandel coast, during his South India wanderings. The town's unique blend of French and Tamil cultures made it a distinctive stop on his South India journey.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references found):

> horrible story that the Lord was here for deluding demons with false doctrines! True indeed, but we are the demons, \
> not those that believed. And just as the Jews denied the Lord Jesus and are since that day wandering over the world \
> as homeless beggars, tyrannised over by everybody, so you are bond-slaves to any nation that thinks it worth while \
> to rule over you. Ah, tyrants! you do not know that the obverse is tyranny, and the reverse slavery. The slave and \
> the tyrant are synonymous. \
> \
> Balaji and G. G. may remember one evening at Pondicherry \'97 we were discussing the matter of sea-voyage with a \
> Pandit, and I shall always remember his brutal gestures and his Kadapi Na (never)!…


---

### R.R. Sabha, Madras

**Location:** Raghunatha Rao's Sabha Hall, Triplicane, Chennai, India
**Period:** 1893
**Significance:** Public lecture before sailing to America


The R.R. Sabha (Raghunatha Rao Sabha) in Triplicane, Madras, was one of the venues where Vivekananda gave his famous lectures before departing for the Parliament of Religions. Madras supporters organized public meetings to raise funds and bid farewell to the Swami.


*No direct reference found in the Complete Works.*


---

### Castle Kernan, Madras

**Location:** Castle Kernan, Triplicane, Chennai, India
**Period:** 1893
**Significance:** Pre-departure gathering


Castle Kernan in Triplicane, Madras, was one of the important residences where Vivekananda stayed and received visitors before his departure to America in 1893. Madras supporters rallied around him during his preparations for the Parliament of Religions.


*No direct reference found in the Complete Works.*


---

### Margao

**Location:** Margao, Margao, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** Wandering through Goa


Margao (Madgaon), the commercial capital of Goa, was visited by Vivekananda during his South India wanderings. His passage through the former Portuguese colony exposed him to the blend of Indian and European cultures unique to Goa.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references found):

---

### Sitakund

**Location:** Sitakund, Chittagong, Sitakund, Pre-Independence India
**Period:** 1890
**Significance:** Ancient Shiva pilgrimage


Sitakund (Chandranath Hills) near Chittagong, one of the most ancient pilgrimage sites in Bengal, was visited by Vivekananda. The hilltop Chandranath Shiva temple and the sacred fire that burns perpetually here held deep religious significance.


*No direct reference found in the Complete Works.*


---

### Sandakphu

**Location:** Sandakphu Peak, Sandakphu, Nepal
**Period:** 1888
**Significance:** Highest point of West Bengal


Sandakphu on the Nepal-India border, the highest peak in West Bengal, was visited by Vivekananda during his early Himalayan wanderings. From here one can see four of the world's five highest peaks. The rarified atmosphere and Himalayan grandeur deeply inspired the young monk.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references found):

> \
> \
> Darjeeling, \
> 23 rd April, 1898. \
> \
> \
> My health was excellent on my return from Sandukphu (11,924 ft.) and other places; but after returning to \
> Darjeeling, I had first an attack of fever, and after recovering from that, I am now suffering from cough and cold. I \
> try to escape from this place every day; but they have been constantly putting it off for a long time. However, \
> tomorrow, Sunday, I am leaving; after halting at Kharsana for a day I start again for Calcutta on Monday. I shall \
> send you a wire after starting. We should hold an annual meeting of the Ramakrishna Mission, and also one for the \
> Math. In both the meetings the accounts of famine relief must be submitted, and…


---

### Bareilly

**Location:** Bareilly, Bareilly, India
**Period:** 1888
**Significance:** North India wandering


Bareilly in the Rohilkhand region of Uttar Pradesh was visited by Vivekananda during his wandering years. The city near the foothills of the Himalayas was a stop on his northward journey through the Gangetic plain.


**From the Complete Works** (4 references found):

> \
> ON THE WAY TO BARAMULLA \
> \
> PERSONS: The Swami Vivekananda, Gurubhais, and a party of Europeans and disciples, amongst whom were Dhira \
> Mata, the "Steady Mother"; one whose name was Jaya; and Nivedita. \
> \
> Place: From Bareilly to Baramulla, Kashmir. \
> \
> Time: June 14 to 20, 1898. \

> [Swami Ramakrishnananda, at the Baranagore Math]. They would not be sent to the care of Balaram Basu, for \
> there would be such nice delays and that would be death to the flowers. I think if sent to Chitpur, to your depot, it \
> would reach you there at the very place; if not, write sharp. Baburam [Swami Premananda] is here, going up in a \
> day or two to Allahabad. I too am going off from this place very soon. I go perhaps to Bareilly and up. What is \
> Balaram Babu [Balaram Bose] doing? \
> \
> My Pranams etc. to you all. \
> \
> \

> audience in English, and another to the Indian residents in Hindi. This was my maiden speech in Hindi, but \

> Captain Sevier, and as soon as it is a little cool, return to the…

> home. If she has no money to go back, give her a passage \'97 even if you have to beg. \

> remains I cannot touch, as that is the expense for the pending lawsuit. \
> \

### Belgaum (Belagavi)

**Location:** Belgaum (Belagavi), Belagavi, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** South India journey


Belgaum (now Belagavi) in northern Karnataka was visited by Vivekananda during his South India journey. The town at the edge of the Deccan plateau was an important stop as he traversed the route between Maharashtra and Karnataka.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references found):

> \
> Bombay, \
> 24th May, 1893. \
> \
> \
> Very glad to receive your letter and that of dear Haripada. Please do not be sorry that I could not write to you very \
> often. I am always praying to the Lord for your welfare. I cannot go to Belgaum now as arrangements are all ready \
> for my starting for America on the 31st next. The Lord willing, I shall see you on returning from my travels in \
> America and Europe. Always resign yourselves to the Lord Shri Krishna. Always remember that we are but \
> puppets in the Lord's hands. Remain pure always. Please be careful not to become impure even in thought, as also \
> in speech and action; always try to do good to others as far as in you lies. And remember that…


---

### Ernakulam (Kochi)

**Location:** Ernakulam, Kochi, Kochi, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** Kerala visit


Ernakulam, the mainland part of Kochi (Cochin), was visited by Vivekananda during his Kerala journey. The gateway to the backwaters and the historic port city of Kerala exposed him to the unique culture and Vedic traditions of this southernmost state.


*No direct reference found in the Complete Works.*


---

### Panaji, Goa

**Location:** Panaji (Panjim), Panaji, India
**Period:** 1892
**Significance:** Goa visit


Panaji, the capital of the former Portuguese colony of Goa, was visited by Vivekananda. He observed the unique Goan culture blending Indian and Portuguese traditions, and visited the famous Basilica of Bom Jesus containing the relics of St. Francis Xavier.


*No direct reference found in the Complete Works.*


---

### Somnath Temple

**Location:** Somnath, Saurashtra, Somnath, India
**Period:** 1891
**Significance:** First Jyotirlinga


Somnath, the first and most revered of the twelve Jyotirlingas of Lord Shiva, was a major pilgrimage destination for Vivekananda during his Gujarat wanderings. He was deeply moved by the historic significance of this temple, repeatedly destroyed and rebuilt over millennia, as a symbol of India's resilience.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references found):

> vitality in religion, but for good or evil it is there; you cannot get out of it, you have it now and for ever, and you \
> have to stand by it, even if you have not the same faith that I have in our religion. You are bound by it, and if you \
> give it up, you are smashed to pieces. That is the life of our race and that must be strengthened. You have \
> withstood the shocks of centuries simply because you took great care of it, you sacrificed everything else for it. \
> Your forefathers underwent everything boldly, even death itself, but preserved their religion. Temple alter temple \
> was broken down by the foreign conqueror, but no sooner had the wave passed than the spire of the temple rose up…

### Puri — Jagannath Dham

**Location:** Jagannath Temple, Puri, Puri, India
**Period:** 1889
**Significance:** One of the four dhams


Puri on the Bay of Bengal, home to the Jagannath Temple and one of the four sacred dhams of Hinduism, was a major pilgrimage destination for Vivekananda. He was deeply moved by the democratic spirit of Jagannath worship that transcended caste distinctions.


**From the Complete Works** (331 references found):

> \
> Others stood beside the Swami Vivekananda, on the same platform as he, as apostles of particular creeds and \
> churches. But it was his glory that he came to preach a religion to which each of these was, in his own words, "only \
> a travelling, a coming up, of different men, and women, through various conditions and circumstances to the same \
> goal". He stood there, as he declared, to tell of One who had said of them all, not that one or another was true, in \
> this or that respect, or for this or that reason, but that "All these are threaded upon Me, as pearls upon a string. \
> Wherever thou seest extraordinary holiness and extraordinary power, raising and purifying humanity, know thou…
