### Lahore

**Location:** Lahore (now Pakistan), Lahore, Pre-Independence India
**Period:** 1897
**Significance:** Major lecture tour


Vivekananda delivered powerful lectures in Lahore (now in Pakistan) during his 1897 tour. He spoke at large public gatherings and the Arya Samaj, urging Hindus to develop strength of character alongside spirituality: 'Be and make.' He was received with great enthusiasm.


**From the Complete Works** (21 references found):

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> THE COMMON BASES OF HINDUISM \
> \
> On his arrival at Lahore the Swamiji was accorded a grand reception by the leaders, both of the Arya Samaj and of \
> the Sanatana Dharma Sabha. During his brief stay in Lahore, Swamiji delivered three lectures. The first of these \
> was on "The Common Bases of Hinduism", the second on "Bhakti" . and the third one was the famous lecture on \
> "The Vedanta" . On the first occasion he spoke as follows: \
> \
> This is the land which is held to be the holiest even in holy Aryavarta; this is the Brahmavarta of which our great \
> Manu speaks. This is the land from whence arose that mighty aspiration after the Spirit, ay, which in times to \

> sinking, sinking, and have come to the last stage to which a human being can come. For what country is there in \
> the world where man has to sleep with the cattle? And for this, blame nobody else, do not commit the mistake of \
> the ignorant. The effect is here and the cause is here too. We are to blame. Stand up, be bold, and take the blame \
> on your own shoulders. Do not go about throwing mud at others; for all the faults you suffer from, you are the sole \
> and only cause. \
> \
> Young men of Lahore, understand this, therefore, this great sin hereditary and national, is on our shoulders. There \
> is no hope for us. You may make thousands of societies, twenty thousand political assemblages,…

> \
> One great prophet, however, arose in the north, Govind Singh, the last Guru of the Sikhs, with creative genius; and \
> the result of his spiritual work was followed by the well-known political organisation of the Sikhs. We have seen \
> throughout the history of India, a spirtitual upheaval is almost always succeeded by a political unity extending over \
> more or less area of the continent, which in its turn helps to strengthen the spiritual aspiration that brings it to \
> being. But the spiritual aspiration that preceded the rise of the Mahratta or the Sikh empire was entirely reactionary. \
> We seek in vain to find in the court of Poona or Lahore even a ray of reflection of that intellectual…

> \
> Just now I received a letter from Gangadhar. It is good news that he is determined to start a centre in that district. \
> Write to him saying that his friend, the Magistrate, has sent an excellent reply to my letter. As soon as we come \
> down to the plains from Kashmir, I shall send back Latu, Niranjan, Dinu, and Khoka. For there is no suitable work \
> for them here any more; also within three to four weeks send Shuddhananda, Sushil, and one other to me. Send \
> them to the house of Mr. Shyamacharan Mukhopadhyaya, Medical Hall, Cantonment, Ambala. From there I shall \
> go to Lahore. They should have each two thick gerua-coloured jerseys, and two blankets for bedding. I shall buy \
> them…

> . . . Captain Sevier says he is very anxious for a site. He wishes to have a spot near Mussoorie or in some other \
> central place, as soon as possible. ... The thing is that we do not want a place which is too cold, at the same time it \
> must not be too hot. Dehra Dun is unbearable in summer, but pleasant in winter; Mussoorie itself is, I dare say, not \
> the right place for many in winter. Above or below it, that is, in British or Garhwal territory, some land is sure to \
> be found. At the same time there must be a supply of water at the place throughout the year, for drinking purposes \
> and for everyday use. My plan is this: With only Achyutananda and Gupta I go from Murree to Rawalpindi,…

### Amritsar

**Location:** Golden Temple area, Amritsar, India
**Period:** 1897
**Significance:** Sikh holy city


Vivekananda visited the sacred city of Amritsar and the Golden Temple (Harmandir Sahib) during his 1897 tour of the Punjab. He was deeply moved by the Sikh tradition of community service (langar) and its principle of equality.


**From the Complete Works** (3 references found):

> To Swami Brahmananda \
> \
> \
> My Dear Rakhal, \
> \
> \
> Amritsar, \
> 2nd September, 1897. \
> \
> \
> Yogen tells me in a letter to buy the house at Baghbazar for Rs. 20,000. Even if we buy that house, there are still a \
> lot of difficulties; for example, we shall have to break it down in part and make the drawing room into a big hall, \
> and similar alterations and repairs. Moreover the house is very old and ramshackle. However, consult Girish Babu \

> well what the people of our country are when it comes to money-matters. . . . On my behalf, you personally talk \
> with the friends there and politely make them understand that it is all right if they can find ways and means to bear \
> the expenses; but if they cannot do so, all of you come back to the Math at Calcutta or go to Rarnnad and establish \
> the Math there. \
> \
> \
> I am now going to the hills at Dharamsala. Niranjan, Dinu, Krishnalal, Latu, and Achyut will stay at Amritsar. Why \
> did you not, all these days, send Sadananda to the Math? If he is still there, then send him to the Punjab on receipt \
> of a letter from Niranjan from Amritsar. I intend to start work in the Punjab after a…

> \
> DEAR Mother, (Shrimati Indumati Mitra) \
> \
> Please be not anxious because I could not write to you and could not go to Belgaon. I was suffering very much \
> from illness and it was impossible for me to go then. Now thanks to my travels in the Himalayas, I have greatly \
> regained my health. I shall soon resume work. In two weeks I am going to the Punjab, and just after delivering a \
> lecture or two at Lahore and Amritsar, I shall start via Karachi for Gujarat, Cutch, etc. I shall surely see you at \
> Karachi. \
> \
> This Kashmir is a veritable heaven on earth. Nowhere else in the world is such a country as this. Mountains and \
> rivers, trees and plants, men and women, beasts and birds \'97 all…

> I hope you and Hari are now in good health. The work in Lahore went off with great eclat. Now I go to Dehra \
> Dun. The Sind tour is postponed. I have yet no news whether Dinu, Latu, and Krishnalal have reached Jaipur. Babu \
> Nagendranath Gupta will collect subscriptions and donations from here and send them to the Math to meet \
> expenses. Send him regular receipts. Let me know if you have received anything from Murree, Rawalpindi, and \

> \
> \
> Reply to me C/o Post Master, Dehra Dun. Other letters you may send me after hearing from me from Dehra Dun. \
> My health is good; only I have to get up at night once or twice. I am having sound sleep; sleep is not…

### Srinagar

**Location:** Dal Lake / Srinagar, Srinagar, India
**Period:** 1897–1898
**Significance:** Spiritual retreat


Vivekananda visited Kashmir in 1897–1898 for spiritual retreat and meditation. He had powerful spiritual experiences here. The goddess Sharada appeared to him in a vision, blessing his work. He was profoundly moved by the beauty of the Vale of Kashmir.

### Dhaka (East Bengal)

**Location:** Dhaka, Dhaka, Pre-Independence India
**Period:** 1901
**Significance:** Eastern Bengal visit


Vivekananda visited Dhaka (then East Bengal, now Bangladesh) in 1901. He delivered lectures and was welcomed by enormous crowds. He encouraged the people of Bengal to combine spirituality with practical service and nation-building.

### Ramakrishna Mission Founded

**Location:** Alambazar Math, Kolkata, India
**Period:** 1 May 1897
**Significance:** Founding of Ramakrishna Mission — historic milestone


On 1 May 1897, Vivekananda convened a meeting at Alambazar Math at which the Ramakrishna Mission was formally founded. Its twin aims: to spread Vedanta for individual liberation, and to serve humanity as manifestations of God — 'Shiva in the form of the poor.'

**Location:** Meerut city, Meerut, India
**Period:** 1897
**Significance:** Lecture tour — North India


Vivekananda visited Meerut as part of his 1897 lecture tour across Northern India. He delivered inspiring addresses on practical Vedanta and the need for a national renewal rooted in spiritual strength.

> suffering for a long time from some pain at the back of my neck. If you can get hold of some very old ghee, then \
> send some of it to him at Dehra Dun and some of it to me also at my Khetri address. You are sure to get it from \
> Habu or Sharat (lawyer). Address it to Babu Raghunath Bhattacharya, Dehra Dun, N.W.P. . . . and it will reach \
> him. \
> \
> The day after tomorrow I am leaving for Saharanpur; from there to Rajputana. \
> \
> \
> Yours affectionately, \
> VlVEKANANDA. \
> \
> \

### Dehra Dun

**Location:** Doon Valley, Dehradun, India
**Period:** 1897
**Significance:** Himalayan foothills retreat


Vivekananda visited Dehradun in the foothills of the Himalayas for rest and recovery after his exhausting Western tour. He rested here while planning further lecture tours and the establishment of new Math centres.


**From the Complete Works** (10 references found):

> To Swami Premananda \
> \
> \
> My DearBaburam, \
> \
> \
> Dehra Dun, \
> 24th November, 1897. \
> \
> \
> I got all news about you from Hariprasanna. I am especially pleased to hear that Rakhal and Hari are now quite \
> well. \
> \

> Lahore, \
> 15th November, 1897. \
> \
> \
> It is a matter of deep regret that in spite of my earnest wishes, I do not find it feasible to go to Karachi this time \
> and see you. First, because Captain and Mrs. Sevier, who have come from England and are travelling with me for \
> the last nine months nearly, are very anxious to buy some land at Dehra Dun and start an orphanage there. It is \
> their special request that I should go and open the work. This makes it unavoidable to go to Dehra Dun. \
> \
> Secondly, owing to my kidney troubles I cannot count upon a long life. Even now it is one of my desires to start a \
> Math in Calcutta, towards which as yet I could do nothing. Moreover, the people of my…

> so that he may present the same to the Raja even on Board the ship, or somewhere in the city of Bombay. Send the \
> superior copy to Khetri. Have this passed in a meeting and if any change is needed, no harm. Then sign it, all of \
> you, only leaving a blank for my name, and I shall sign it on going to Khetri. Let no pains be spared in this. \
> \
> . . . Captain Sevier says he is very anxious for a site. He wishes to have a spot near Mussoorie or in some other \
> central place, as soon as possible. ... The thing is that we do not want a place which is too cold, at the same time it \
> must not be too hot. Dehra Dun is unbearable in summer, but pleasant in winter; Mussoorie itself is, I dare say,…

> See that there is no remissness whatever in the medical treatment of Yogen \'97 if necessary spend money by \
> drawing on the capital. Did you go and meet Bhavanath's wife? \
> \
> If Brahmachari Hariprasanna can come, it will be very helpful. Mr. Sevier has become very impatient about \
> acquiring a house somewhere; it will be good if something is done quickly about it! Hariprasanna is an engineer; so \
> he will be able to do something quickly about it. Also he understands better about the suitability of places. They \
> (the Seviers) like to have a place somewhere near about Dehra Dun or Mussoorie; that is to say, the place must not \
> be too cold and must be habitable throughout the year. So send…

> \
> \
> Lahore, \
> 11th November, 1897. \
> \
> \
> The lecture at Lahore is over somehow. I shall start for Dehra Dun in a day or two. I have now postponed my tour \
> to Sind, as none of you are agreeable to it, and also because of various other obstacles. Somebody has opened my \
> two letters from England on the way. So don't send me letters any further for the present. Send them after I have \
> written for them from Khetri. If you go to Orissa, then make arrangements that some one will do all the work as \
> your representative \'97 say Hari, especially now, when I am daily expecting letters from America. \
> \
> Perhaps the will that I asked you to make in favour of Hari and Sharat has now been made. \

### Kathiawar

**Location:** Kathiawar region, Gujarat, Rajkot, India
**Period:** 1897
**Significance:** Revisiting Gujarat


Vivekananda revisited Gujarat on his 1897 all-India tour, reconnecting with old patrons and supporters and establishing new contacts for the Ramakrishna Mission's expanding work.


**From the Complete Works** (11 references found):

> Dehra Dun, \
> 24th November, 1897. \
> \
> \
> I have duly received your letter and that of dear Haripada. Of course you have ample reason to feel sorry for, but \
> you see, I couldn't help it. And what took me here also became a fiasco; neither could I go to Sindh. It is the Lord's \
> will. Now, I have an idea of proceeding to Calcutta through Rajputana, Kathiawar, and Sindh. But some difficulty \
> may crop up on the way. If all goes well, I am certainly coming to Sindh. You must have undergone a lot of \
> difficulty in coming to Hyderabad by arranging for leave etc. Any least trouble undergone, is bound to produce its \
> excellent results. Friday next I shall leave this place, and have a mind to go…

> acquiring a house somewhere; it will be good if something is done quickly about it! Hariprasanna is an engineer; so \
> he will be able to do something quickly about it. Also he understands better about the suitability of places. They \
> (the Seviers) like to have a place somewhere near about Dehra Dun or Mussoorie; that is to say, the place must not \
> be too cold and must be habitable throughout the year. So send Hariprasanna at once straight to Sj. Shyamapada \
> Mukherjee, Medical Hall, Ambala Cantonment. As soon as I go down to the Punjab, I shall send Mr. Sevier along \
> with him. I am returning (to the Math) in a trice after a tour of the Punjab, Karachi, and then via Rajputana, not via…

> Lord. \
> \
> Recently my health was very bad, and so I have been delayed, otherwise I had intended to leave for the Punjab this \
> week. The doctor had advised me not to go to the plains at the present time, as it is very hot there. Perhaps I may \
> reach Karachi by about the last week of October. Now I am doing somewhat well. There is nobody else with me \
> now excepting two American friends \'97 ladies. Probably I shall part from them at Lahore. They will wait for me in \
> Calcutta or in Rajputana. I shall probably visit Cutch, Bhuj, Junagad, Bhavnagar, Limbdi, and Baroda and then \
> proceed to Calcutta. My present plan is to go to America via China and Japan in November or December, but it is…

> 14th December, 1897. \
> \
> \
> I have today sent your power of attorney with my signature. . . . Draw the money as early as you can, and wire to \
> me as soon as you have done so. A Raja of a place in Bundelkhand named Chatrapur has invited me. I shall visit \
> the place on my way to the Math. The Raja of Limbdi, too, is writing earnestly. I cannot avoid going there also. I \
> shall make a lightning tour of Kathiawar \'97 that is what it will come to. I shall feel great relief as soon as I reach \
> Calcutta. . . . There is no news from Boston as yet; perhaps Sharat is coming; anyway, whenever any news comes \
> from anywhere, write to me immediately. \
> \
> \
> Yours affectionately, \
> VlVEKANANDA. \

> now. His brother, the one just next to him in age, is the Commander-in-Chief. Efforts are being made to arrange a \
> lecture under his chairmanship. I shall write all about this afterwards. If the meeting for the lecture is held in a day \
> or two, I shall stay back, otherwise I go out again on my travels. Sevier is still at Murree. His health is very bad \'97 \
> going about in the jolting tongas and jutkas. The Bengali gentlemen of Murree are very good and courteous. Give \
> my respects to G. C. Ghosh, Atul, Master Mahashay, and others, and keep up the spirits of everybody. What is the \
> news about the house which Yogen suggested we should buy? In October I shall go down from here and shall…

### Hyderabad — Mission Work

**Location:** Hyderabad city, Hyderabad, India
**Period:** 1897
**Significance:** Establishing Vedanta centre


Vivekananda returned to Hyderabad as part of his mission-building tour, where he helped establish a Ramakrishna Mission branch and encouraged local leaders to apply Vedanta principles to social service.


**From the Complete Works** (5 references found):

> Neutral Line, Poona, \
> 15th June, 1892. \
> \
> \
> It is a long time since I heard from you. I hope I have not offended you anyway. I came down with the Thakore \
> Saheb of Mahabaleshwar, and I am living here with him. I would remain here a week or more and then proceed to \
> Rameshwaram via Hyderabad. \
> \
> Perhaps by this time every hitch has been removed from your way in Junagad; at least I hope so. I am very anxious \
> to leam about your health, especially that sprain, you know. \
> \
> I saw your friend the Surti tutor to the Prince of Bhavnagar. He is a perfect gentleman. It was quite a privilege to \
> make his acquaintance; he is so good and noble-natured a man. \

> \
> Another of us was a physician. He thought of a sentence from a German medical book. It was written on his paper. \
> \
> Several days later I went to this man again, thinking possibly I had been deluded somehow before. I took other \
> friends, and on this occasion also he came out wonderfully triumphant. \
> \
> Another time I was in the city of Hyderabad in India, and I was told of a Brahmin there who could produce \
> numbers of things from where, nobody knew. This man was in business there; he was a respectable gentleman. \
> \
> And I asked him to show me his tricks. It so happened that this man had a fever, and in India there is a general \
> belief that if a holy man puts his hand on a sick man he…

> \
> To Shri Alasinga Perumal \
> \
> \
> C/o Babu Madhusudan Chattopadhyaya \
> Superintending Engineer \
> Khartabad, Hyderabad, \
> 11th February, 1893. \
> \
> Dear Alasinga, \
> \
> Your friend, the young graduate, came to receive me at the station, so also a Bengali gentleman. At present I am \
> living with the Bengali gentleman; tomorrow I go to live with your young friend for a few days, and then I see the \

> \

> excellent results. Friday next I shall leave this place, and have a mind to go via Saharanpur to Rajputana direct. I…

> \
> My great love to you and all others. Yesterday Sharat returned to the Math from Darjeeling \'97 his health is much \
> better than it was before. I have come here after a tour of East Bengal and Assam. All work has its ups and downs, \
> its periods of intensity and slac kn ess. Again it will rise up. What fear? .... \
> \
> Whatever that may be, I say that you stop your work for some time and come straight back to the Math. After you \
> have taken a month's rest here, you and I together will make a grand tour via Gujarat, Bombay, Poona, Hyderabad, \
> Mysore to Madras. Would not that be grand? If you cannot do this, stop your lectures in Madras for a month. Take \
> a little good food and sleep well.…

### Rose Bank, Darjeeling

**Location:** Rose Bank Cottage, Darjeeling, India
**Period:** 1897
**Significance:** Rest & recuperation


Rose Bank was a cottage in Darjeeling where Vivekananda stayed to rest and recuperate during his return to India. The cool Himalayan climate helped restore his health after the rigors of the first Western tour.


**From the Complete Works** (36 references found):

> To Swami Ramakrishnananda \
> \
> \
> Dear Shashi, \
> \
> \
> Darjeeling, \
> 20th April, 1897. \
> \
> \
> All of you have doubtless reached Madras by this time. I should think Biligiri is certainly taking great care of you, \
> and that Sadananda serves you as your attendant. In Madras the worship should be done in a completely Sattvic \
> manner, without a trace of Rajas in it. I hope Alasinga has by now returned to Madras. Don't enter into wrangles \

> \
> I began to take a lot of exercise on horseback, both morning and evening. Since that I am very much better indeed. \
> I was so much better the first week of my gymnastics that I have scarcely felt so well since I was a boy and used to \
> have kusti (wrestling) exercises. I really began to feel that it was a pleasure to have a body. Every movement made \
> me conscious of strength \'97 every movement of the muscles was pleasurable. That exhilarating feeling has \
> subsided somewhat, yet I feel very strong. In a trial of strength I could make both G. G. and Niranjan go down \
> before me in a minute. In Darjeeling I always felt that I was not the same man. Here I feel that I have no disease…

> The Hindus are making processions and all that, but they cannot give money. The only help I got in the world was \
> in England, from Miss Muller, and Mr. Sevier. I thought there that a thousand pounds was sufficient to start at least \
> the principal centre in Calcutta, but my calculation was from the experience of Calcutta ten or twelve years ago. \
> Since then the prices have gone up three or four times. \
> \
> The work has been started anyhow. A rickety old little house has been rented for six or seven shillings, where \
> about twenty-four young men are being trained. I had to go to Darjeeling for a month to recover my health, and I \
> am glad to tell you I am very much better, and would you…

> \'ab Chronology \
> \
> \'ab Addressee \
> \
> THE EDUCATION THAT INDIA NEEDS \
> \
> (Written to Shrimati Sarala Ghosal, B.A., Editor, Bharati, from Darjeeling, 24th April, 1897. (Translated from Bengali .) \
> \
> In reply to your questions about the methods of work, the most important thing I have to say is that the work \
> should be started on a scale which would be commensurate with the results desired. I have heard much of your \
> liberal mind, patriotism, and steady perseverance from my friend Miss Muller; and the proof of your erudition is \
> evident. I look upon it as a great good fortune that you are desirous to know what little this insignificant life has \
> been able to attempt; I shall state it to…

> and that alone which prospers, virtue always suffers. It seems, however advanced the Western nations are in \
> scientific culture, they are mere babies in metaphysical and spiritual education. Material science can only give \
> worldly prosperity, whilst spiritual science is for eternal life. If there be no eternal life, still the enjoyment of \
> spiritual thoughts as ideals is keener and makes a man happier, whilst the foolery of materialism leads to \
> competition and undue ambition and ultimate death, individual and national. \
> \
> This Darjeeling is a beautiful spot with a view of the glorious Kanchenjanga (28,146 ft.) now and then when the \
> clouds permit it, and from a near hilltop one can…

**Significance:** Retreat


Dharamagarh estate near the Advaita Ashrama in Mayavati was a property associated with the Sevier family who established the Ashrama. Vivekananda visited this area during his 1901 pilgrimage to the Himalayas.

> THE ADVAITA ASHRAMA, HIMALAYAS \

> In Whom is the Universe, Who is in the Universe, Who is the Universe; in Whom is the Soul, Who is in the Soul, \

> brings an end to misery and leads to Infinite Freedom. Wherever there has been expansion in love or progress in \

> To Swami Swarupananda, editor o/Prabuddha Bharata, Mayavati \

> My dear S[warupananda], \

> [March 1899\\ \

> 14th June 1902. \

> \
> Your letters had to wait a few days, as I was out of town in a village.! Well, many thanks for all the information I \
> got. Mr. Okakura [Kakuzo] has been to the Math, but I was away. He will be in Calcutta a few weeks more and \
> then goes to Bombay. He intends taking a house near the city to leam intimately the customs of Bengalees. I am so \
> glad to leam Margo's [Sister Nivedita's] intention to stop at Mayavati longer. She really requires good rest, and she \
> had none in Europe, I am sure of that. If she were amenable to my advice as of old, I would take away every book \
> and every scrap of paper from her, make her walk some, eat a lot and sleep a lot more. As to…

> (These lines there sent in a letter . March, 1899, by Swamiji, for embodying in the prospectus of the Advaita Ashrama, \
> \
> Mayavati, Almora, Himalayas.) \
> \
> In Whom is the Universe, Who is in the Universe, Who is the Universe; in Whom is the Soul, Who is in the Soul, \
> Who is the Soul of Man; knowing Him \'97 and therefore the Universe \'97 as our Self, alone extinguishes all fear, \
> brings an end to misery and leads to Infinite Freedom. Wherever there has been expansion in love or progress in \
> well-being, of individuals or numbers, it has been through the perception, realisation, and the practicalisation of the \
> Eternal Truth \'97 THE ONENESS…

> \'ab \

> CXXXIV \
> \
> To Swami Swarupananda, editor o/Prabuddha Bharata, Mayavati \

> \
> My dear S[warupananda], \
> \
> \
> [March 1899\\ \

> 14th June 1902. \
> \
> \
> Your letters had to wait a few days, as I was out of town in a village.! Well, many thanks for all the information I \
> got. Mr. Okakura [Kakuzo] has been to the Math, but I was away. He will be in Calcutta a few weeks more and \
> then goes to Bombay. He intends taking a house near the city to leam intimately the customs of Bengalees. I am so \
> glad to leam Margo's [Sister Nivedita's] intention to stop at Mayavati longer. She really requires good rest, and she \
> had none in Europe, I am sure of that. If she were amenable to my advice as of old, I would take away every book \
> and every scrap of paper from her, make her walk some, eat a lot and sleep a lot more. As to…

### Captain Sevier's Samadhi, Mayavati

**Location:** Advaita Ashrama, Mayavati, Mayavati, India
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Samadhi/memorial of Captain Sevier


Captain James Henry Sevier, one of Vivekananda's most devoted Western disciples, passed away at the Advaita Ashrama in Mayavati in 1900. His samadhi (memorial tomb) is located within the Ashrama grounds.


**From the Complete Works** (15 references found):

> Nivedita as Swami Vivekananda translated them orally for some of his Western disciples during a Himalayan \
> pilgrimage in 1898. \

> California by Miss Josephine MacLeod, shortly before her passing away in 1948. This footnoted handwritten \
> version was first published in the collection of poetry entitled In Search of God and Other Poems (Mayavati: \
> Advaita Ashrama, 1968). \
> \

### Sister Nivedita's House, Almora

**Location:** Almora town, Almora, India
**Period:** 1898
**Significance:** Nivedita's Almora residence


The house in Almora where Sister Nivedita (Margaret Noble) stayed during Vivekananda's 1898 Himalayan tour. This was part of her introduction to Indian life and spirituality under Vivekananda's direct guidance.


**From the Complete Works** (111 references found):

> \
> 10. Answering the remark of a disciple who felt that it would be better for her to come back to this life again and \
> again and help the causes that were of interest to her instead of striving for personal salvation with a deep longing \
> to get out of life, the Swami retorted quickly: "That\'92s because you cannot overcome the idea of progress. But things \
> do not grow better. They remain as they are; and we grow better by the changes we make in them." \
> \
> 11. It was in Almora that a certain elderly man, with a face full of amiable weakness, came and put him a question \
> about Karma. What were they to do, he asked, whose Karma it was to see the strong oppress the weak? The Swami \
> turned…

### Lala Badri Shah's House, Almora

**Location:** Almora, Almora, India
**Period:** 1898–1901
**Significance:** Devoted patron's home


Lala Badri Shah was a devoted patron and supporter of Vivekananda in Almora. His house was a regular halt for Vivekananda during his Himalayan visits, providing hospitality and support for the Swami's work.

### Vivekananda's Cottage, Almora

**Location:** Almora hill station, Almora, India
**Period:** 1898–1901
**Significance:** Primary residence in Almora


The cottage in Almora that served as Swami Vivekananda's primary residence during his multiple visits to the hill station. He conducted spiritual discourses and received visitors here, and composed some of his writings during this period.


**From the Complete Works** (111 references found):

> do not grow better. They remain as they are; and we grow better by the changes we make in them." \
> \

> about Karma. What were they to do, he asked, whose Karma it was to see the strong oppress the weak? The Swami \
> turned…

### Thompson House, Almora

**Location:** Almora, Almora, India
**Period:** 1898
**Significance:** Residence of Western disciples


Thompson House in Almora was a residence associated with Western disciples visiting Vivekananda in the Himalayas. It was part of the circle of homes and cottages used by the Swami and his growing international community during the Himalayan sojourns.

### Kasar Devi Temple, Almora

**Location:** Kasar Devi Hill, Almora, India
**Period:** 1898
**Significance:** Sacred meditation site


The ancient Kasar Devi temple on a ridge above Almora was visited by Vivekananda for meditation. The location is famous for its unique electromagnetic energy. Vivekananda meditated here and declared it a powerful spiritual spot.


**From the Complete Works** (111 references found):

> \

> do not grow better. They remain as they are; and we grow better by the changes we make in them." \
> \

> about Karma. What were they to do, he asked, whose Karma it was to see the strong oppress the weak? The Swami \
> turned…

### Syahi Devi Temple, Almora

**Location:** Syahi Devi, Almora, India
**Period:** 1898
**Significance:** Temple visit


Syahi Devi temple near Almora was visited by Vivekananda during his Himalayan travels. The forested hilltop shrine of Goddess Syahi Devi was a traditional pilgrimage destination in the Kumaon region.

### Sri Raghunath Temple, Almora

**Location:** Almora bazaar, Almora, India
**Period:** 1898
**Significance:** Temple visit


The historic Sri Raghunath temple in Almora's bazaar area was visited by Vivekananda during his stay in the hill town. He revered Lord Rama and visited Vaishnava shrines throughout his wandering years.

### Dewaldhar Estate, Almora

**Location:** Dewaldhar, Almora, India
**Period:** 1898
**Significance:** Rest & retreat

### Bhim Tal

**Location:** Bhimtal Lake, Bhimtal, India
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Himalayan retreat


The picturesque lake town of Bhimtal in the Kumaon Himalayas was visited by Vivekananda during his final Himalayan journey. The serene lake setting provided an ideal environment for meditation.


*No direct reference found in the Complete Works.*


---

### Roshanara Bagh, Delhi

**Location:** Roshanara Road, Delhi, India
**Period:** 1898
**Significance:** Delhi camp during return tour


Roshanara Bagh in north Delhi was associated with Vivekananda's visits to Delhi during his post-return Indian tour. The Mughal-era garden was a landmark meeting point where he addressed gatherings of devotees and seekers.


**From the Complete Works** (16 references found):

> To Swami Brahmananda \
> \
> \
> MyDear Rakhal, \
> \
> \
> Delhi, \
> 8th December, 1897. \
> \
> \
> We shall start for Khetri tomorrow. Gradually the luggage has greatly increased. After Khetri I intend to send \
> everybody to the Math. I could get done through them none of the work which I had hoped. That is to say, it is \
> quite certain that none of them can do anything if he always remains with me. Unless each goes about \

> To Swami Brahmananda \
> \
> \
> My Dear Rakhal, \
> \

Dharamshala in the Kangra valley of Himachal Pradesh was visited by Vivekananda during his northern India tour. The town, set against the backdrop of the Dhauladhar range, was a refreshing hill station where he met devotees and rested.

### Ambala

**Location:** Ambala, Ambala, India
**Period:** 1897
**Significance:** North India tour stop


Ambala was visited by Vivekananda during his northern India tour after his return from America. An important railway junction and military cantonment, it was a transit stop as he traveled between Delhi and Punjab.


**From the Complete Works** (7 references found):

> has written to me to inform the Reception Committee to take some money for expenses from the amount that is left \

> audience in English, and another to the Indian residents in Hindi. This was my maiden speech in Hindi, but \

> "nigger"! "Oh, how wonderful!" and that sort of thing. Next Saturday there will be another lecture for the \
> Europeans. A big Association has been set on foot here \'97 let us wait and see how far it works in future. The object \
> of the Association is to impart education and religion. \

> Mukherjee, Medical Hall, Ambala Cantonment. As soon as I go down to the Punjab, I shall send Mr. Sevier along \

### Pampore (Pampur)

**Location:** Pampore, Kashmir Valley, Pampore, India
**Period:** 1898
**Significance:** Kashmir valley tour


Pampore, the saffron town of Kashmir, was visited by Vivekananda during his 1898 Kashmir journey. Famous for its vast saffron fields, the town lies in the heart of the Kashmir valley south of Srinagar.


*No direct reference found in the Complete Works.*

### Anant Nag

**Location:** Anantnag, Anantnag, India
**Period:** 1898
**Significance:** Kashmir pilgrimage


Anantnag in south Kashmir, historically known as Islamabad, was visited by Vivekananda during his 1898 Kashmir pilgrimage. The town is known for the Shiva Linga at Martand Sun Temple nearby and the sacred spring of Anantnag.


*No direct reference found in the Complete Works.*

### Baramula

**Location:** Baramulla (Varahamula), Baramulla, India
**Period:** 1898
**Significance:** Gateway to Kashmir


Baramulla (ancient Varahamula), the gateway to the Kashmir valley on the Jhelum river, was a stop during Vivekananda's Kashmir tour. The town has ancient Hindu temple ruins and is a significant entry point to the valley.

### Jammu

**Location:** Jammu city, Jammu, India
**Period:** 1898
**Significance:** Vaishno Devi gateway


Jammu, the 'City of Temples,' was visited by Vivekananda as part of his Kashmir journey. The winter capital of J&K was the starting point for pilgrimages to Vaishno Devi and provided access to the Kashmir valley via the Banihal pass.

### Amarnath cave temple

**Location:** Amarnath Cave, Himalaya, Amarnath, India
**Period:** 1898
**Significance:** Holiest Shiva pilgrimage


Vivekananda undertook the arduous pilgrimage to the Amarnath Cave in 1898, one of the holiest shrines of Lord Shiva. At an altitude of 3,888m, he had a profound mystical experience before the ice Shiva Lingam. He described it as one of the most powerful spiritual experiences of his life.

### Dal Lake (Dalh Lake)

**Location:** Dal Lake, Srinagar, India
**Period:** 1898
**Significance:** Houseboat stay, Kashmir


Vivekananda stayed on a houseboat on the famous Dal Lake in Srinagar during his 1898 Kashmir visit. He gave teachings to his Western disciples Nivedita, Josephine MacLeod and Sara Bull from his Dal Lake houseboat, creating an unforgettable atmosphere of spiritual instruction against the backdrop of the Himalayas.

Achabal, with its magnificent Mughal garden and the spring of the Achabal Nag, was visited by Vivekananda during his south Kashmir tour. The natural spring flowing through terraced gardens was a site of natural beauty that the Swami appreciated.

Vivekananda visited Shillong, then the capital of Assam and a picturesque hill station, in 1901. He rested in this pleasant town in the Khasi hills and gave discourses to the local community during his last major tour of eastern India.

> nothing but that, the throbbing of the beauty of the Vedas and Upanishads". . . . \
> \
> From Rawalpindi to Murree we went by tonga, and there we spent some days before setting out for Kashmir. Here \
> the Swami came to the conclusion that any effort which he might make to induce the orthodox to accept a \
> European as a fellow-disciple, or in the direction of woman's…

> To Swami Brahmananda \
> \
> \
> My Dear Rakhal, \
> \
> \
> Murree, \
> 12th October, 1897. \
> \
> \
> I wrote at length in yesterday's letter. I think it desirable to give you special directions about certain matters. ... (1) \
> To all those who collect money and send it to the Math . . . the acknowledgment of the amounts will be issued from \
> the Math. (2) The acknowledgment must be in duplicate, one for the sender, and one for filing in the Math. (3) \

> To Swami Brahmananda \
> \
> \
> My Dear Rakhal, \
> \
> \
> Murree, \
> 11th October, 1897. \
> \
> \
> I feel I have been working as if under an irresistible impulse for the last ten days, beginning from Kashmir. It may \
> be either a physical or a mental disease. Now I have come to the conclusion that I am unfit for further work. ... I \
> now understand that I have been very harsh to all of you. But I knew, however, that you would bear with all my \

*— lectures, perhaps, in the Punjab, otherwise none. The people of our country have not yet offered me even as much \*

> BHAKTI \
> \
> (.Delivered at Sialkote, Punjab ) \
> \
> In response to invitations from the Punjab and Kashmir, the Swami Vivekananda travelled through those parts. He \
> stayed in Kashmir for over a month and his work there was very much appreciated by the Maharaja and his \
> brothers. He then spent a few days in visiting Murree, Rawalpindi, and Jammu, and at each of these places he \
> delivered lectures. Subsequently he visited Sialkote and lectured twice, once in English and once in Hindi. The \
> subject of the Swamiji's Hindi lecture was Bhakti, a summary of which, translated into English, is given below: \
> \
> The various religions that exist in the world, although they differ in the form of…

> \
> I hope your health is now quite all right. Since reaching Dharamsala I have been all right. I like the cold places; \
> there the body keeps well. I have a desire either to visit a few places in Kashmir and then choose an excellent site \
> and live a quiet life there, or to go on floating on the water. I shall do what the doctor advises. The Raja is not here \
> now. His brother, the one just next to him in age, is the Commander-in-Chief. Efforts are being made to arrange a \
> lecture under his chairmanship. I shall write all about this afterwards. If the meeting for the lecture is held in a day \
> or two, I shall stay back, otherwise I go out again on my travels. Sevier is still at Murree. His…

### Sialkot

**Location:** Sialkot city, Sialkot, Pre-Independence India
**Period:** 1897
**Significance:** Punjab tour


Sialkot, an ancient city in Punjab (now Pakistan), was visited by Vivekananda during his 1897 Punjab tour. The city near the foothills of the Himalayas has deep historical roots, and he addressed gatherings of devotees there.


**From the Complete Works** (2 references found):

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> BHAKTI \
> \
> (.Delivered at Sialkote, Punjab ) \
> \
> In response to invitations from the Punjab and Kashmir, the Swami Vivekananda travelled through those parts. He \
> stayed in Kashmir for over a month and his work there was very much appreciated by the Maharaja and his \
> brothers. He then spent a few days in visiting Murree, Rawalpindi, and Jammu, and at each of these places he \
> delivered lectures. Subsequently he visited Sialkote and lectured twice, once in English and once in Hindi. The \
> subject of the Swamiji's Hindi lecture was Bhakti, a summary of which, translated into English, is given below: \

> \
> \
> I hope you and Hari are now in good health. The work in Lahore went off with great eclat. Now I go to Dehra \
> Dun. The Sind tour is postponed. I have yet no news whether Dinu, Latu, and Krishnalal have reached Jaipur. Babu \
> Nagendranath Gupta will collect subscriptions and donations from here and send them to the Math to meet \
> expenses. Send him regular receipts. Let me know if you have received anything from Murree, Rawalpindi, and \
> Sialkot. \
> \
> \
> Reply to me C/o Post Master, Dehra Dun. Other letters you may send me after hearing from me from Dehra Dun. \
> My health is good; only I have to get up at night once or twice. I am having sound sleep; sleep is not spoiled even \
> after…

### Captain Sevier's Bungalow, Mayavati

**Location:** Advaita Ashrama Campus, Mayavati, India
**Period:** 1898–1900
**Significance:** Home of Captain Sevier at Mayavati


The bungalow of Captain James Henry Sevier at the Advaita Ashrama, Mayavati. Captain Sevier, one of Vivekananda's most devoted Western disciples, worked tirelessly to establish the Ashrama and lived here until his death in October 1900. He laboured so hard clearing the tea-estate grounds that he died prematurely, and was cremated by the nearby Sarada River according to Hindu rites.

> glad to leam Margo's [Sister Nivedita's] intention to stop at Mayavati longer. She really requires good rest, and she \
> had none in Europe, I am sure of that. If she were amenable to my advice as of old, I would take away every book \
> and every scrap of paper from her, make her walk some, eat a lot and sleep a lot more. As to…

### Captain Sevier's Point, Mayavati

**Location:** Captain Sevier's Point, Mayavati, India
**Period:** 1898–1900
**Significance:** Himalayan viewpoint


A viewpoint within the Mayavati estate named in memory of Captain James Henry Sevier, who along with Mrs. Charlotte Sevier co-founded the Advaita Ashrama at Vivekananda's inspiration. The point offers panoramic views of the Himalayan range, a landscape Sevier loved deeply. Dharamagarh, the highest hill within the Mayavati boundaries, affords the finest views of the snow range from this area.

### Sister Nivedita cottage (Oakley House)

**Location:** Oakley House, Almora, Almora, India
**Period:** 1898
**Significance:** Western disciples' residence at Almora


Oakley House in Almora — now known as Nivedita House — was the residence where Vivekananda's Western disciples stayed during the May 1898 Himalayan visit. Sara Bull, Josephine MacLeod, Sister Nivedita and Mrs. Patterson lodged here while Vivekananda stayed nearby at Lala Badri Shah's house and visited every morning for breakfast. It was under a tree at Oakley House that Vivekananda blessed Sister Nivedita, helping her overcome distraction in meditation. Here Nivedita first truly learned the art of meditation under Swamiji's guidance.


**From the Complete Works** (111 references found):

> to get out of life, the Swami retorted quickly: "That\'92s because you cannot overcome the idea of progress. But things \
> do not grow better. They remain as they are; and we grow better by the changes we make in them." \

> about Karma. What were they to do, he asked, whose Karma it was to see the strong oppress the weak? The Swami \

### Swami Vivekananda memorial rest hall

**Location:** Almora town, Almora, India
**Period:** 1897, 1898
**Significance:** Memorial to Vivekananda's stays in Almora


The Swami Vivekananda Memorial Rest Hall in Almora commemorates the Swami's repeated stays in the hill town during his lecture tours and Himalayan retreats. Vivekananda visited Almora in 1897 after his triumphant return from the West and again in 1898 with Sister Nivedita and other Western disciples. The hall serves as a gathering place for devotees and preserves the memory of these transformative Himalayan sojourns.

### Government Inter College

**Location:** Government Inter College, Almora, Almora, India
**Period:** 27 July 1897
**Significance:** First Hindi lecture by Vivekananda


On 27 July 1897, Vivekananda delivered a public lecture at the Government Inter College, Almora, on the subject 'Vedic Teaching in Theory and Practice' — marking the very first time Swamiji addressed an audience in Hindi. The lecture was a landmark event in his post-Western tour of India. A statue of Vivekananda and a commemorative photograph were later unveiled at the college in his honour. The college hall was subsequently proposed to be named 'Swami Vivekananda Sabhagaar'.

### Stimpson Mansion

**Location:** Stimpson Mansion, Los Angeles, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Hosted Vivekananda in Los Angeles


The Stimpson Mansion in Los Angeles was among the grand residences where Swami Vivekananda was received during his 1900 visit to Southern California. His second Western tour brought him to California's growing cities, where he attracted devoted followers among the spiritually-minded residents of Los Angeles.


**From the Complete Works** (29 references found):

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> THE OPEN SECRET \
> \
> (.Delivered at Los Angeles, Calif., 5th Januaiy 1900 ) \
> \
> Whichever way we turn in trying to understand things in their reality, if we analyse far enough, we find that at last \
> we come to a peculiar state of things, seemingly a contradiction: something which our reason cannot grasp and yet \
> is a fact. We take up something \'97 we know it is finite; but as soon as we begin to analyse it, it leads us beyond our \
> reason, and we never find an end to all its qualities, its possibilities, its powers, its relations. It has become infinite. \
> Take even a common flower, that is finite enough; but who is there that can say he knows all about the flower? \

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> CHRIST, THE MESSENGER \
> \
> \{Delivered at Los Angeles, California, 1900 ) \
> \
> The wave rises on the ocean, and there is a hollow. Again another wave rises, perhaps bigger than the former, to \
> fall down again, similarly, again to rise \'97 driving onward. In the march of events, we notice the rise and fall, and \
> we generally look towards the rise, forgetting the fall. But both are necessary, and both are great. This is the nature \
> of the universe. Whether in the world of our thoughts, the world of our relations in society, or in our spiritual \
> affairs, the same movement of succession, of rises and falls, is going on. Hence great predominances in the march \

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> THE POWERS OF THE MIND \
> \
> (.Delivered at Los Angeles, California, January 8, 1900) \
> \
> All over the world there has been the belief in the supernatural throughout the ages. All of us have heard of \
> extraordinary happenings, and many of us have had some personal experience of them. I would rather introduce the \
> subject by telling you certain facts which have come within my own experience. I once heard of a man who, if any \
> one went to him with questions in his mind, would answer them immediately; and I was also informed that he \
> foretold events. I was curious and went to see him with a few friends. We each had something in our minds to ask, \

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> WORK AND ITS SECRET \
> \
> (.Delivered at Los Angeles, California, January 4, 1900 ) \
> \
> One of the greatest lessons I have learnt in my life is to pay as much attention to the means of work as to its end. \
> He was a great man from whom I learnt it, and his own life was a practical demonstration of this great principle I \
> have been always learning great lessons from that one principle, and it appears to me that all the secret of success is \
> there; to pay as much attention to the means as to the end. \
> \

> Rs. 5,000 of that money. I am not going to take more. I had paid back Rs. 2,000 or more of that Rs. 5,000 in India. \
> But it seems, Brahmananda, wanting to show as much of the Rs. 35,000 intact as he could, drew upon my Rs. \
> \
> 2,000; so I owe them Rs. 5,000 still on that score. \
> \
> Anyway, I thought I could make money here in California and pay them up quietly. Now I have entirely failed in \
> California financially. It is worse here than in Los Angeles. They come in crowds when there is a free lecture and \
> very few when there is something to pay. \
> \
> I have some hopes yet in England. It is necessary for me to reach England in May. There is not the least use in \
> breaking my health in San…

### Roxie Blodgett Cottage

**Location:** Roxie Blodgett Cottage, Los Angeles, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Home of a devoted follower in Los Angeles


The cottage of Roxie Blodgett in Los Angeles was a place where Swami Vivekananda was welcomed and hosted during his 1900 California visit. Such devoted American followers provided him with comfortable stays and helped organise his lecture engagements in Southern California during his second Western tour.

> This morning I shall try to present to you some ideas about breathing and other exercises. We have been discussing \

> line. Five persons in this country will join their heads together and say, "We will have a joint-stock company", and \
> in five hours it is done; in India they could not do it in fifty years; they are so unpractical in matters…

> \'ab \

> WOMEN OF INDIA \
> \
> \
> (.Delivered at the Shakespeare Club House, in Pasadena, California, on January 18, 1900 ) \
> \
> SWAMI VlVEKANANDA: "Some persons desire to ask questions about Hindu Philosophy before the lecture and to \
> question in general about India after the lecture; but the chief difficulty is I do not know what I am to lecture on. I \
> would be very glad to lecture on any subject, either on Hindu Philosophy or on anything concerning the race, its \
> history, or its literature. If you, ladies and gentlemen, will suggest anything, I would be very glad." \
> \

*— Lectures and Discourses \*

> Indian poets, the Yavanika (Ionian or Greek) influence on the whole of the dramatic literature of the time is \
> ascertained, then one should first stop to compare whether the Aryan dramas are at all like the Greek. Those who \
> have studied the mode of action and style of the dramas of both the languages must have to admit that any such \
> likeness, if found, is only a fancy of the obstinate dreamer, and has never any real existence as a matter of fact. \
> Where is that Greek chorus? The Greek Yavanika is on one side of the stage, the Aryan diametrically on the other. \
> The characteristic manner of expression of the Greek drama is one thing, that of the Aryan quite another. There is \
> not the…

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> THE RAMAYANA \
> \
> (.Delivered at the Shakespeare Club, Pasadena, California, January 31, 1900 ) \
> \
> There are two great epics in the Sanskrit language, which are very ancient. Of course, there are hundreds of other \
> epic poems. The Sanskrit language and literature have been continued down to the present day, although, for more \
> than two thousand years, it has ceased to be a spoken language. I am now going to speak to you of the two most \
> ancient epics, called the Ramayana and the Mahabharata. They embody the manners and customs, the state of \
> society, civilisation, etc., of the ancient Indians. The oldest of these epics is called Ramayana, "The Life of Rama". \

> No search has been dearer to the human heart than that which brings to us light from God. No study has taken so \
> much of human energy, whether in times past or present, as the study of the soul, of God, and of human destiny. \
> However immersed we are in our daily occupations, in our ambitions, in our work, in the midst of the greatest of \

> \

> \
> \

> ourselves smaller and smaller, and dissociating ourselves, contrary to the plans laid down our scriptures. \
> \

### First Unitarian Church

**Location:** First Unitarian Church, Oakland, USA

Swami Vivekananda lectured at the First Unitarian Church in Oakland, California, during his 1900 West Coast visit. Oakland's Unitarian congregation, known for its progressive and intellectually open approach to religion, provided an ideal audience for his teachings on the universality of Vedanta and the common spiritual core of all religions.


**From the Complete Works** (16 references found):

> \
> \
> "Swami Vivekananda's second trip to the West occurred in 1899-1900. During the first half of 1900 he worked in \
> and around San Francisco, California. I was a resident of that city, twenty-two years old at the time. ... I heard him \
> lecture perhaps a score of times from March to May of 1900, and recorded seventeen of his talks. ... \
> \
> "The lectures were given in San Francisco, Oakland, and Alameda, in churches, in the Alameda and San Francisco \
> Homes of Truth, and in rented halls. ... Altogether Swamiji gave, besides nearly daily interviews and informal \
> classes, at least thirty or forty major addresses in March, April, and May. ... \
> \
> "I was long hesitant about transcribing and…

> such a manner as to place the faith of Brahma before all. The all-pervading thought and leading principle of the \
> Hindoo religion is the inherent divinity of the soul; the soul is perfect, and religion is the manifestation of divinity \
> already existing in man. The present is merely a line of demarkation between the past and future, and of the two \
> tendencies in man, if the good preponderates he will move to a higher sphere, if the evil has power, he degenerates. \
> These two are continually at work within him; what elevates him is virtue, that which degenerates is evil. \
> \
> Kananda will speak at the First Unitarian Church tomorrow morning. \

> "Brahminism" in all its subtle attraction, because of its embodiment of ancient and truthful principles, was the \
> subject which held an audience in closest attention last evening at the First Unitarian Church [Minneapolis], while \
> Swami Vive Kananda expounded the Hindoo faith. It was an audience which included thoughtful women and men, \
> for the lecturer had been invited by the "Peripatetics," and among the friends who shared the privilege with them \
> were ministers of varied denominations, as well as students and scholars. Vive Kananda is a Brahmin priest, and he \
> occupied the platform in his native garb, with caftan…

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> THE CLAIMS OF VEDANTA ON THE MODERN WORLD \
> \
> (.Report of a lecture delivered in Oakland on Sunday, February 25, 1900, with \
> editorial comments of the Oakland Enquirer) \
> \
> The announcement that Swami Vivekananda, a distinguished savant of the East, would expound the philosophy of \
> Vedanta in the Parliament of Religions at the Unitarian Church last evening, attracted an immense throng. The \
> main auditorium and ante-rooms were packed, the annexed auditorium of Wendte Hall was thrown open, and this \
> was also fdled to overflowing, and it is estimated that fully 500 persons, who could not obtain seats or standing \

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> THE PEOPLE OF INDIA \
> \
> (.Report of a lecture delivered in Oakland on Monday, March 19, 1900, with \
> editorial comments of the Oakland Enquirer) \
> \
> The lecture which the Swami Vivekananda gave Monday night in his new course on "The People of India", was \
> interesting, not only for what he had to relate of the people of that country, but for the insight into their mental \
> attitude and prejudices which the speaker gave without really meaning it. It is apparent that the Swami, educated \
> and intellectual man that he is, is no admirer of Western civilisation. He has evidently been a good deal embittered \


---

### George H.Perry house

**Location:** George H.Perry house, Alameda, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Hosted Vivekananda in Alameda, California


The home of George H. Perry in Alameda, California, was one of the Bay Area residences where Swami Vivekananda was hosted during his 1900 California visit. Alameda, situated on an island in San Francisco Bay, was home to several of his devoted California followers who welcomed him warmly during his second Western tour.


**From the Complete Works** (7 references found):

> \
> \
> "Swami Vivekananda's second trip to the West occurred in 1899-1900. During the first half of 1900 he worked in \
> and around San Francisco, California. I was a resident of that city, twenty-two years old at the time. ... I heard him \
> lecture perhaps a score of times from March to May of 1900, and recorded seventeen of his talks. ... \
> \
> "The lectures were given in San Francisco, Oakland, and Alameda, in churches, in the Alameda and San Francisco \
> Homes of Truth, and in rented halls. ... Altogether Swamiji gave, besides nearly daily interviews and informal \
> classes, at least thirty or forty major addresses in March, April, and May. ... \
> \
> "I was long hesitant about transcribing and…

> Swami Vivekananda (turning quietly to her): You say that, and you have seen me? \
> \
> A GREETING \
> \
> \{New Discoveries, Vol. 6, p. 136.) \
> \
> From Mr. Thomas Allan's reminiscences of Swami Vivekananda's visit to Alameda, California, 1900: \
> \
> Mr. Allan: Well, Swami, I see you are in Alameda! \
> \
> Swami Vivekananda: No, Mr. Allan, I am not in Alameda; Alameda is in me. \
> \
> "THIS WORLD IS A CIRCUS RING" \

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> THE PRACTICE OF RELIGION \
> \
> (.Delivered at Alameda, California, on April 18, 1900*) \
> \
> We read many books, many scriptures. We get various ideas from our childhood, and change them every now and \
> then. We understand what is meant by theoretical religion. We think we understand what is meant by practical \
> religion. Now I am going to present to you my idea of practical religion. \
> \
> We hear all around us about practical religion, and analysing all that, we find that it can be brought down to one \

> To an American friend \
> \
> \
> Mother is becoming propitious once more. Things are looking up. They must. \
> \
> \
> Alameda, California, \
> 12th April, 1900. \
> \
> \
> Work always brings evil with it. I have paid for the accumulated evil with bad health. I am glad. My mind is all the \
> better for it. There is a mellowness and a calmness in life now, which was never there before. I am learning now \
> how to be detached as well as attached, and mentally becoming my own master. . . . \

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> THE PRACTICE OF RELIGION \
> \
> \{At Alameda, Calif., March 18, 1900) \
> \
> We read many books, but that does not bring us knowledge. We may read all the Bibles in the world, but that will \
> not give us religion. Theoretical religion is easy enough to get, any one may get that. What we want is practical \
> religion. \
> \
> The Christian idea of a practical religion is in doing good works \'97 worldly utility. \


---

### Home of Truth

**Location:** Home of Truth, San Francisco, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** New Thought spiritual centre in San Francisco


The Home of Truth in San Francisco was one of the key New Thought centres associated with Swami Vivekananda's activities during his 1900 California visit. San Francisco's vibrant spiritual scene embraced his Vedantic teachings, and the Home of Truth network provided organised venues and dedicated audiences for his lectures and classes.


**From the Complete Works** (84 references found):

> \
> MOHAMMED \
> \
> This article was recorded by Ida Anseli in shorthand. As, however, Swamiji's speed was too great for her in her early days, dots are put in the \
> articles to indicate the omissions, while the words within square brackets are added by way of linking up the disconnected parts. \
> \
> (.Delivered on March 25, 1900, in the San Francisco Bay Area) \
> \
> The ancient message of Krishna is one harmonising three \'97 Buddha's, Christ's and Mohammed\'92s. Each of the three \
> started an idea and carried it to its extreme. Krishna antedates all the other prophets. [Yet, we might say,] Krishna \
> takes the old ideas and synthesises them, [although] his is the most ancient message. His message…

> \
> THE SOUL AND GOD \
> \
> This article was recorded by Ida Anseli in shorthand. As, however, Swamiji's speed was too great for her in her early days, dots are put in the \
> articles to indicate the omissions, while the words within square brackets are added by way of linking up the disconnected parts. \
> \
> (Delivered in San Francisco, March 23, 1900) \
> \
> Whether it was fear or mere inquisitiveness which first led man to think of powers superior to himself, we need not \
> discuss. ... These raised in the mind peculiar worship tendencies, and so on. There never have been [times in the \
> history of mankind] without [some ideal] of worship. Why? What makes us all struggle for something beyond what…

*— poems of love. In all Sanskrit books, the writers first of all salute their Gurus. So he saluted that girl as his first \*

> \
> THE GITA I \
> \
> This article was recorded by Ida Anseli in shorthand. As, however, Swamiji's speed was too great for her in her early days, dots are put in the \
> articles to indicate the omissions, while the words within square brackets are added by way of linking up the disconnected parts. \
> \
> (.Delivered in San Francisco, on May 26, 1900) \
> \
> To understand the Gita requires its historical background. The Gita is a commentary on the Upanishads. The \
> Upanishads are the Bible of India. They occupy the same place as the New Testament does. There are [more than] \
> a hundred books comprising the Upanishads, some very small and some big, each a separate treatise. The \
> Upanishads do not reveal…

> \
> THE GITA II \
> \
> This article was recorded by Ida Anseli in shorthand. As, however, Swamiji's speed was too great for her in her early days, dots are put in the \
> articles to indicate the omissions, while the words within square brackets are added by way of linking up the disconnected parts. \
> \
> (.Delivered in San Francisco, on May 28, 1900) \
> \
> The Gita requires a little preliminary introduction. The scene is laid on the battlefield of Kurukshetra. There were \
> two branches of the same race fighting for the empire of India about five thousand years ago. The Pandavas had the \
> right, but the Kauravas had the might. The Pandavas were five brothers, and they were living in a forest. Krishna…

> \
> "Learn to be the witness. If two dogs are fighting on the street and I go out there, I get mixed up in the fight; but if \
> I stay quietly in my room, I witness the fight from the window. So leam to be the witness." (Ibid.) \
> \
> \
> \
> 34. From Mr. Thomas J. Allan 's reminiscences of a private talk with Swami Vivekananda in San Francisco, \
> California, 1900: \
> \
> "We do not progress from error to truth, but from truth to truth. Thus we must see that none can be blamed for \
> what they are doing, because they are, at this time, doing the best they can. If a child has an open razor, don\'92t try to \
> take it from him, but give him a red apple or a brilliant toy, and he will drop the razor. But he…

### Camp Irving

**Location:** Camp Irving, Camp Taylor, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Summer retreat camp in Marin County, California


Camp Irving at Camp Taylor in Marin County, California, was a retreat destination associated with Swami Vivekananda's 1900 California visit. The redwood forests and peaceful natural setting of Marin County provided an ideal environment for meditation and the informal spiritual gatherings that Vivekananda favoured during his time in the San Francisco Bay Area.


*No direct reference found in the Complete Works.*

### Rue Anatole

**Location:** Rue Anatole, Perros-Guirec, France
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Address in Brittany where Vivekananda rested


Rue Anatole in Perros-Guirec, a coastal town in Brittany, France, was the address where Swami Vivekananda rested during his stay in this picturesque corner of northern France in 1900. The rugged Breton coastline and peaceful atmosphere of Perros-Guirec offered him much-needed respite during his second Western tour.


*No direct reference found in the Complete Works.*

### Mont Blanc

**Location:** Mont Blanc, Chamonix, France
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Europe's highest peak, visited by Vivekananda


Swami Vivekananda visited the Mont Blanc massif near Chamonix during his 1900 European travels. The majestic alpine scenery of the highest peak in the Alps deeply impressed him, and his time in the mountains of Chamonix provided both physical refreshment and spiritual inspiration during his second and final Western tour.


*No direct reference found in the Complete Works.*

### Mont-Saint-Michel

**Location:** Mont-Saint-Michel, Mont-Saint-Michel, France
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Historic island abbey visited by Vivekananda in Normandy


Swami Vivekananda visited the iconic Mont-Saint-Michel in Normandy, France, during his 1900 European travels. The ancient Benedictine abbey perched on a tidal island in the English Channel left a deep impression on him, and he marvelled at the devotion and architectural genius that had produced this magnificent monument of medieval Christian spirituality.

### Istanbul (Constantinople), Turkey

**Location:** Istanbul, Istanbul, Turkey
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Historic city visited by Vivekananda on his return journey


Swami Vivekananda visited Istanbul (then known as Constantinople) during his travels in 1900. The city, straddling Europe and Asia, with its magnificent mosques, bazaars, and the grandeur of the former Byzantine and Ottoman empires, fascinated him deeply. He saw in this great city a living symbol of the meeting of East and West that was so central to his own vision of universal civilisation.

### Vienna, Austria

**Location:** Vienna, Vienna, Austria
**Period:** 1896
**Significance:** Capital of Austria visited during European travels


Swami Vivekananda visited Vienna, the imperial capital of Austria-Hungary, during his European travels in 1896. The city of Beethoven, Mozart, and Freud was a centre of European intellectual and artistic life, and Vivekananda was deeply interested in engaging with the cultural and philosophical currents of fin-de-siècle Vienna.

### Athens, Greeze

**Location:** Athens, Athens, Greece
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Ancient capital of Greece visited by Vivekananda


Swami Vivekananda visited Athens, the cradle of Western civilisation, during his 1900 European and Middle Eastern travels. The Acropolis, the Parthenon, and the philosophical legacy of Socrates, Plato, and Aristotle resonated deeply with him, and he saw in ancient Greek philosophy important parallels with the non-dualistic tradition of Advaita Vedanta.


**From the Complete Works** (60 references found):

> foremost and most obvious manifestation of Prana is the breathing motion. Prana acts upon air, and not air upon it. \
> Controlling the breathing motion is pranayama. Pranayama is practised to get mastery over this motion; the end is \
> not merely to control the breath or to make the lungs strong. That is Delsarte, not Pranayama. These Pranas are the \
> vital forces which manipulate the whole body, while they in their turn are manipulated by other organs in the body, \
> which are called mind or internal organs. So far so good. The psychology is very clear and most precise; and yet it \
> is the oldest rational thought in the world! Wherever there is any philosophy or rational thought, it owes…

> \
> So much is this the case that it has become a truism. \
> \
> But fortunately we must inquire into the beyond. This present, this expressed, is only one part of that unexpressed. \
> The sense universe is, as it were, only one portion, one bit of that infinite spiritual universe projected into the plane \
> of sense consciousness. How can this little bit of projection be explained, be understood, without, knowing that \
> which is beyond? It is said of Socrates that one day while lecturing at Athens, he met a Brahmin who had travelled \
> into Greece, and Socrates told the Brahmin that the greatest study for mankind is man. The Brahmin sharply \
> retorted: "How can you kn ow man until you kn ow Gods"…

*— Lectures from Colombo to Almora \*

> man can marry a woman for money, and, after capturing her dollars, can kick her out. In India, on the contrary, \
> when a woman marries for money, her children are considered slaves, according to our teaching, and when a rich \
> man marries, his money passes into the hands of his wife, so that he would be scarcely likely to turn the keeper of \
> his money out of doors. \
> \
> You say we are heathens, we are uneducated, uncultivated, but we laugh in our sleeves at your want of refinement \
> in telling us such things. With us, quality and birth make caste, not money. No amount of money…

### Amsterdam, Holland

**Location:** Amsterdam, Amsterdam, Netherlands
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Dutch capital visited during European travels


Swami Vivekananda visited Amsterdam in Holland during his 1900 European travels. The prosperous Dutch capital, with its rich tradition of religious tolerance and philosophical inquiry — home to Spinoza's pantheistic philosophy — provided a stimulating environment for his own reflections on the unity of all existence central to Vedanta.


**From the Complete Works** (5 references found):

> Just weigh the matter in your mind. Those uncared-for lower classes of India \'97 the peasants and weavers and the \
> rest, who have been conquered by foreigners and are looked down upon by their own people \'97 it is they who from \
> time immemorial have been working silently, without even getting the remuneration of their labours! But what \
> great changes are taking place slowly, all over the world, in pursuance of nature's law! Countries, civilisations, and \
> supremacy are undergoing revolutions. Ye labouring classes of India, as a result of your silent, constant labours \
> Babylon, Persia, Alexandria, Greece, Rome, Venice, Genoa, Baghdad, Samarqand, Spain, Portugal, France, \
> Denmark,…

> October 6, 1896 \
> \
> Dear Christina, \
> \
> I am sure you got my letter from Switzerland. \
> \
> I am now in London, back after having travelled through Germany and Holland. \
> \
> How are things going with you? Had you a nice summer? How are you physically and spiritually? How is Mrs. \
> Fhunkey [Funke] and all the other friends? Have you any news of Baby?* Where is Kr [Kripananda] and what is \
> he doing now? \
> \
> I have another Sannyasin over here with me now, who will work here whilst I am away to India, where I go this \

> Professor of Philosophy in the University of Kiel. I have seen professors of Sanskrit in America and in Europe. \
> Some of them are very sympathetic towards Vedantic thought. I admire their intellectual acumen and their lives of \
> unselfish labour. But Paul Deussen \'97 or as he prefers to be called in Sanskrit, Deva-Sena \'97 and the veteran Max \
> Muller have impressed me as being the truest friends of India and Indian thought. It will always be among the most \
> pleasing episodes in my life \'97 my first visit to this ardent Vedantist at Kiel, his gentle wife who travelled with him \
> in India, and his little daughter, the darling of his heart \'97 and our travelling together through Germany…

> LI \
> \
> To Mr. E. T. Sturdy \
> \
> \
> C/o Miss MacLeod, \
> Hotel Hollande, \
> Rue de La Paix, \
> Paris, \
> \
> 5th September, 1895. \
> \
> Dear and Blessed Friend, \

> I was so, so bad in health in India. My heart went wrong all the way \'97 what with mountain climbing, bathing in \
> glacier water and nervous prostration! I used to get terrible fits [of asthma] \'97 the last lasting about seven days and \
> nights. All the time I was suffocating and had to stand up. \
> \
> This trip has almost made a new man of me. I feel much better and, if this continues, hope to be quite strong before \
> I reach America. How are you? What are you doing? Write everything about yourself, c/o E. T. Sturdy Esq., 25 \
> Holland Villas Road, London, W. \
> \
> With everlasting love and blessings, \
> \
> \
> Ever yours in the Lord, \
> \


---

### Geneva

**Location:** Geneva, Geneva, Switzerland
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Swiss city visited during European travels


Swami Vivekananda visited Geneva during his 1900 European travels. The cosmopolitan Swiss city on the shores of Lac Léman, long a centre of international diplomacy and humanitarian ideals, appealed to his own vision of universal brotherhood. He spent time here resting and meeting with European admirers of his teachings.


**From the Complete Works** (1 references found):

> \
> July 7, 1896 \
> \
> \
> [On the] 18th of this month I start for Switzerland for a holiday. I will come back to London again to work in the \
> Autumn. The work in England bids fair to be much better and deeper than in the U.S. And here in London is the \
> heart of India also. Where are you now? I am passing through Geneva on my way to the Hills. I will be there a day \
> or two. \
> \
> \
> If you be somewhere near, I will make it a point to come to see you. Did you hear Annie Besant? How did you like \
> her? What about your plans of going to India next winter? What about the innocents (Mary and Harriet Hale and Isabelle \
> and Harriet McKindley.) at home? I haven't had any news of them. My love to Father…


---

### Zermatt, Switcherland

**Location:** Zermatt, Zermatt, Switzerland
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Alpine resort below the Matterhorn, visited by Vivekananda


Swami Vivekananda visited Zermatt in the Swiss Alps during his 1900 European travels. The dramatic alpine village beneath the Matterhorn offered him the grandeur of the high mountains that always stirred his spiritual sensibility. The Swiss Alps reminded him of the Himalayas of his homeland, and he spent time here in rest and meditation.


*No direct reference found in the Complete Works.*


---

### Saas Fee, Switzerland

**Location:** Saas-Fee, Saas-Fee, Switzerland
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Alpine village in Switzerland visited by Vivekananda


Saas-Fee, the 'Pearl of the Alps' in the Swiss canton of Valais, was visited by Swami Vivekananda during his 1900 Swiss sojourn. This high alpine village surrounded by glaciers and peaks over 4,000 metres offered him a place of deep tranquillity and natural splendour during the last major journey of his life.

### Lucerne, Switzerland

**Location:** Lucerne, Lucerne, Switzerland
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Swiss city visited during European travels


Swami Vivekananda visited Lucerne in central Switzerland during his 1900 European travels. The picturesque city on Lake Lucerne, with its famous Chapel Bridge and panoramic views of the Alps, charmed him greatly. He rested here between his engagements in other European cities during his second and final Western tour.

---

### Schaffhausen, Switzerland

**Location:** Schaffhausen, Schaffhausen, Switzerland
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Swiss town near Rhine Falls visited by Vivekananda


Schaffhausen in northern Switzerland, famous for the nearby Rhine Falls — the largest waterfall in Europe — was visited by Swami Vivekananda during his 1900 European travels. The thundering power of the Rhine Falls deeply impressed him, and the experience of this natural spectacle resonated with his contemplative spirit.


*No direct reference found in the Complete Works.*


---


## Phase: Second Western Tour (1899–1900)

*Second visit to the West — USA, England, France (Congress of Religions, 1900) and Egypt — despite deteriorating health. Established Vedanta Society of San Francisco and deepened contacts in Paris before returning via Cairo.*


---

### San Francisco

**Location:** Vedanta Society of San Francisco, San Francisco, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Founded Vedanta Society of San Francisco


During his second visit to America, Vivekananda established the Vedanta Society of San Francisco in 1900. He lectured extensively in the Bay Area and attracted devoted students who went on to carry Vedanta in the American West. The society remains active today.

### Los Angeles

**Location:** Los Angeles, California, Los Angeles, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** California lecture tour


Vivekananda lectured in Los Angeles during his 1900 California visit, drawing large audiences. His lectures on practical Vedanta and Jnana Yoga were warmly received by Southern California's spiritually curious population.


**From the Complete Works** (29 references found):

> \
> \
> \'ab \
> \
> THE OPEN SECRET \
> \
> (.Delivered at Los Angeles, Calif., 5th Januaiy 1900 ) \
> \
> Whichever way we turn in trying to understand things in their reality, if we analyse far enough, we find that at last \
> we come to a peculiar state of things, seemingly a contradiction: something which our reason cannot grasp and yet \
> is a fact. We take up something \'97 we know it is finite; but as soon as we begin to analyse it, it leads us beyond our \
> reason, and we never find an end to all its qualities, its possibilities, its powers, its relations. It has become infinite. \
> Take even a common flower, that is finite enough; but who is there that can say he knows all about the flower? \

**Location:** Pasadena, California, Pasadena, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** California lectures


Vivekananda gave several lectures in Pasadena during his California tour in 1900. Mrs. Kate Sanborn and other wealthy Californians became his devotees and supporters of the Vedanta work on the West Coast.

### Camp Taylor (Shanti Ashrama)

**Location:** San Antonio Valley, California, Camp Taylor, USA
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Spiritual camp retreat


Vivekananda's favourite retreat in America. He called this place 'Shanti Ashrama' (Abode of Peace) in California's San Antonio Valley. He spent time here in meditation and teaching, living simply in the hills and finding it reminiscent of India's Himalayas.


*No direct reference found in the Complete Works.*


---

### Paris

**Location:** Congress of Religions, Paris, Paris, France
**Period:** August 1900
**Significance:** Congress of Religions — second Parliament


Vivekananda attended and spoke at the Congress of History of Religions in Paris in August 1900, held alongside the World's Fair. He also spent time with French intellectuals and scholars, and visited the Louvre. He met Jules Bois and other notable Parisian thinkers.


**From the Complete Works** (154 references found):

**Location:** Egyptian Museum / Old Cairo, Cairo, Egypt
**Period:** 1900
**Significance:** Return to India — transit


Vivekananda visited Cairo on his return journey from the West in 1900. He explored the Pyramids and ancient Egyptian ruins with great curiosity, reflecting on the parallels between Egyptian and Hindu spiritual traditions.
